visits.text: Each winter, the Sea of Okhotsk off Abashiri covers with drifting ice plates (ryuhyo) pushed from the Amur River mouth in Siberia - a journey of over 1,000 kilometers. These plates reach Japanese coasts from mid-January to late March. The Aurora icebreaker, with 450-passenger capacity, cleaves these plates with its weight and power during an hour-long cruise. The physical sensation of the ship ramming against ice is unique. Seals laze on the plates, white-tailed sea eagles soar above.

The former Abashiri Prison, built in the Meiji era to clear Hokkaido with convicts, is now a high-quality open-air museum. Five original wooden buildings, classified heritage, recount prison life at the world's edge. Neighboring Mount Tento (207 m) offers from its summit a panoramic view of the city, Lakes Abashiri and Notoro, and the Sea of Okhotsk.

activities.text: In season (mid-January to early April): board the Aurora icebreaker from Ryuhyo-Kaido pier, 5 minutes by car from JR Abashiri Station. The ship makes 4 to 5 crossings daily. Bundle up well - sea wind on deck is biting. If conditions are exceptional and ice thick, the ship's shocks against pack ice can make you lose balance.

Off-season or in addition, the Ryuhyo Norokko panoramic train (January to March) runs along the coast for 1 hour between Abashiri and Shiretoko-Shari, with views of the ice sea from the windows. An extended stop at Kitahama Station, the Japanese rail network's closest to the Sea of Okhotsk, allows seeing ice plates meters away.

Outside ice season, Abashiri remains interesting for its prison-museum, seafood market (specialties: scallops, king crab, Abashiri Zangi - marinated and fried salmon), and Mount Tento hikes. The Okhotsk Ryuhyo Museum, at the mountain's foot, recreates an ice room at -15°C.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: Mount Asahidake peaks at 2,291 meters - it's Hokkaido's rooftop and the island's highest point. It belongs to the Daisetsuzan volcanic group, called "Kamui-mintara" ("playground of the gods") by the Ainu. The ropeway from Asahidake Onsen ascends in 10 minutes to Sugatami Station at 1,600 meters - a large 101-passenger gondola. There begins a world of alpine tundra devoid of trees: sulfurous fumaroles, crater ponds, and a 1-hour circular trail accessible even to non-hikers. In summer, Aleutian dryas and mountain blue heather briefly bloom on the rocky slopes.

Sugatami Pond, visible from the ropeway station, reflects the smoking silhouette of Asahidake's summit in its calm waters. It's one of the park's most photographed sites. Asahidake is also Japan's first place to display autumn colors - mid-September generally - several weeks before the rest of the archipelago.

For experienced hikers, the summit ascent from the ropeway station takes about 2 hours. The trail is rocky and exposed to wind; hiking boots and warm clothing are essential even in August. Once at the summit, the view extends across the entire Daisetsuzan range. Beware of brown bears: a bell and noise are recommended.

activities.text: Take the ropeway early morning, ideally at opening. The 30 to 60-minute circular trail around Sugatami Station passes active fumaroles releasing sulfurous vapors - a reminder that you're on a living volcano. The crater ponds Kagami-ike and Suribachi-ike are accessible on this circuit.

Observe Hokkaido deer and chipmunks (small striped squirrels) frequently visible around the upper trails, especially early morning. Pikas (whistling lagomorphs) inhabit the rocky scree above the tree line - a rare species difficult to observe elsewhere in Japan.

Enjoy the onsen at Asahidake hot spring resort in the evening. This village counts only a dozen buildings including wooden lodges and a few large hotels offering hot spring baths. It's one of Hokkaido's most isolated and wildest onsen - steps from the national park trails.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: Asahiyama Zoo — Revolutionary design zoo opened in 1967, innovative installations allowing observation of animals' natural behaviors, underwater tunnel with seals, polar bear enclosure with immersive dome, penguins walking freely in winter, red pandas gibbons wolves, 2nd most visited zoo in Japan after Ueno, educational and conservation mission, natural hilly forest setting.

Kamikawa Shrine — Shinto shrine built in 1893 at the confluence of the Ishikari and Chubetsu rivers, spiritual guardian of Asahikawa city, imposing vermillion torii, traditional cedar wood architecture, annual matsuri festival in September, cherry trees lining access path blooming in late April, serene local atmosphere not touristy, view of Mount Ohiradake.

Takasago Sake Museum — Historic sake brewery founded in 1899 still in operation, red brick building in Western style classified as heritage, exhibition on traditional brewing process using Hokkaido rice and pure snowmelt water, free tasting of six award-winning sakes, extensive shop, izakaya restaurant serving local cuisine ideal accompaniment, warm atmosphere.

activities.text: Asahikawa ramen tour — Taste the local shoyu ramen style pioneer of Hokkaido since 1947, double pork-fish soup broth with aromatic soy sauce, thin wavy noodles, grilled chashu, six to eight famous restaurants concentrated in Asahikawa Ramen Village, unique family variations, nostalgic Showa atmosphere, generous portions comforting against winter cold.

Night walk at Snow Crystal Museum — Discover unique thematic museum dedicated to snow crystals research of Dr. Nakaya native of Asahikawa, whimsical ice castle architecture, hexagonal hall with 200 macro photographs of snowflakes, temperature maintained at minus 20 degrees, video projection of crystal formation, ice princess dresses rental for photos, fascinating Japanese kitsch.

visits.text: Ashikaga Flower Park (94,000m²) is Japan's largest wisteria garden—over 350 trees, including a wisteria more than 160 years old classified as a natural monument of Tochigi Prefecture. CNN ranked it among the "10 dream destinations in the world" in 2014. The great wisteria's trellis covers 1,000m² (equivalent to 600 tatami mats) and produces about 80,000 flower clusters in violet. The white wisteria tunnel stretches for 80 meters—when you walk through in May, the sweet floral scent is intoxicating. Blooming from late April to mid-May: first the pinks, then violets, whites, and finally yellows.

While you're in Ashikaga, visit Ashikaga Gakkō, considered Japan's oldest school (founded in the 9th century according to some sources). The wooden building, with its reconstructed classrooms and library of Confucian texts, offers a rare glimpse into education in feudal Japan.

activities.text: Stay past sunset: the park's nighttime illuminations (Night Wisteria) transform the wisteria into luminous cascades. The show has been certified as "Japanese Night View Heritage." If you come in winter (late October to late January), the Flower Fantasy illuminations with over 5 million LEDs replace the flowers—it's one of Kanto's three biggest illumination events.
Try the wisteria ice cream (fuji soft cream), a house specialty of the park—violet, lightly perfumed, sold at counters inside the park. It's become Ashikaga's signature culinary souvenir.

visits.text: The Blue Pond of Shirogane (Aoiike) is a curiosity born by accident: built in 1988-1989 as a mudflow control reservoir related to Mount Tokachi eruptions, it accumulated waters laden with colloidal aluminum hydroxide from the nearby Shirahige waterfalls. This aluminum diffracts light and produces the unreal cobalt blue color. The pond's bottom, whitened by sulfide, further accentuates the effect. Color varies throughout the day and with weather: in calm wind and clear sky early morning, the blue is most saturated. In 2012, Apple chose a photo of this pond as wallpaper for OS X Mountain Lion - since then, its fame is global. Dead larches and birches stand in the water, swimming is forbidden (water laden with aluminum).

The Patchwork Road, northwest of Biei, takes its name from the appearance of agricultural fields seen from the heights: potatoes, wheat, beets, and corn form rectangles of textures and colors changing with seasons. In winter, the snowy undulations of these hills are unmatched. At the summit of Hokusei Hill, the observation pyramid offers 360° views of this entire landscape. The Panorama Road, further south, runs along flower gardens open from spring to autumn.

Shikisai no Oka, a flower park at the entrance to Panorama Road, displays dozens of floral varieties in colored bands on a south-facing hill. Tractors and quads offer tours of the estate. From May to October, blooms succeed without interruption - lavender, cosmos, dahlias, sage.

activities.text: Go to the Blue Pond early morning and in calm weather: it's the sine qua non condition for maximum color and perfect reflection in ripple-free water. Tourists will flood in from 9:30-10am. Parking is paid on-site. In winter (November to April), nighttime illuminations transfigure the site - ice blocks under spotlights create an almost surreal effect.

Drive the Patchwork Road stopping at improvised lookouts on the ridges. Bike rental shops in Biei offer mountain bikes and electric bikes to explore secondary paths between fields - it's the most authentic way to taste this rolling countryside reminiscent of central European hills.

Detour to Shirahige Falls, 2.5 km from the Blue Pond: water surges directly from volcanic rock fissures and plunges into the Biei River. The phenomenon is visible from a bridge in the Shirogane hot spring village. The whitish water that gushes turns blue upon joining the river - this is where the aluminum coloring the Blue Pond comes from.

days_plan.itin_title: Hokkaido's Summer Road Trip: Furano Lavender, Shikotsu-Tōya Parks & Coast
Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: Chatan's American Village is a complex built on the former Hamby military airfield, returned in 1981. Opening from 1998 onward, it blends American pop architecture with Okinawan culture. It's a real neighborhood where American families from nearby Kadena Air Base mingle with tourists. In the evening, the buildings light up—rooftop bars, terraces facing the sea.
Sunset Beach, at the foot of American Village, lives up to its name: facing due west, it's one of Okinawa's best sunset spots. About 600 meters of sand with showers and barbecue facilities.

Cape Zanpa in Yomitan (20-minute drive): 30-meter coral cliffs with a 31-meter white lighthouse—one of the few climbable lighthouses in Japan. 360-degree views of Aguni and Kume Islands. At the entrance, the Zanpa Dai-Shisa measures 8.75 meters—Japan's largest shisa statue.
Yachimun no Sato in Yomitan emerged when wood-fired kilns were banned in Naha in the 1970s. Over 60 workshops produce traditional yachimun—you can freely enter and buy directly from artisans.

activities.text: Settle onto a rooftop bar at Depot Island to watch the sun sink into the sea. The vibe blends awamori cocktails with taco rice—Okinawa's star fusion dish.
Climb Cape Zanpa's lighthouse via the spiral staircase—the view is worth the effort. Below, Zanpa Beach is perfect for a quick swim.

days_plan.itin_title: Okinawa and Ryukyu Islands Journey: Beaches and Culture

visits.text: Chichibu's Hitsujiyama Park transforms from mid-April to early May into a carpet of moss phlox (shibazakura)—over 400,000 plants of 9 varieties blanket the gentle slopes in pink, white, purple, and red. On clear days, Mount Bukō rises in the background. It's the spring equivalent of Provence's lavender fields, Japanese-style. The Shibazakura festival draws large crowds—come on a weekday if possible.

At Nagatoro, the Arakawa River has sculpted the Iwadatami ("stone tatami") rock formations over 500 meters, classified as a National Natural Monument. These crystalline schist slabs, stacked like giant tatami mats, date back several hundred million years. From the bridge above the river, the view is spectacular—especially in autumn with maples ablaze.

In winter only (January-February): the ice stalactites of Misotsuchi form a wall 30 meters wide and 10 meters high along a cliff. In the evening, they're illuminated in colors (red, blue, yellow, orange)—an ephemeral spectacle lasting only a few weeks.

activities.text: At Nagatoro, operators offer boat descents (nagabune) on the Arakawa River through the rock formations—the ride lasts about 20 minutes and passes through light rapids. It's playful and refreshing in summer. If you prefer more adrenaline, rafting is also offered on certain sections.
Before leaving Nagatoro, try kakko dango, grilled rice dumplings brushed with sweet soy sauce—a local specialty sold in shops along the river.

days_plan.itin_title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey

visits.text: Fukuroda Falls (120m high, 73m wide) ranks among Japan's three most famous waterfalls, alongside Nachi Falls (Wakayama) and Kegon Falls (Nikko). Water cascades down four rocky tiers—hence the alternative name "Yodo-no-Taki" (Four-Stage Falls). A 276-meter tunnel carved through rock leads to a multi-level observation platform: at the bottom, you stand meters from the base; at the top (elevator access), you command panoramic views of all four tiers. The monk Saigyo Hōshi supposedly said one must visit once each season to grasp its full beauty—and he was right: verdant spring, cool summer refuge, flaming autumn foliage (early November), frozen ice curtains in winter (late December–February).

If you're in the area, push on to Ryūjin Suspension Bridge in Hitachiota—it spans 375 meters across the Ryūjin Dam at 100 meters height. It's one of Japan's longest pedestrian bridges. Local operators offer bungee jumping here if you're feeling brave.

activities.text: In winter (November to January), nighttime illuminations transform the falls and access path into a sound-and-light spectacle. If you come in autumn (early November), combine the falls with an overnight at a ryokan in the small hot spring town of Daigo—the atmosphere is peaceful and the onsen excellent.

A short hiking trail (20-30 minutes, steep) leads from the tunnel exit to Namase Falls, less crowded. If you have more energy, continue 45 additional minutes to a small temple dedicated to Kannon, the Goddess of Mercy, hidden in the mountains.

places.name: Fukaura

visits.text: Aoike Pond ('blue pond') is the jewel of Juniko - the twelve lakes of the Shirakami-Sanchi virgin forest, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1993 as one of the world's most extensive massifs of primitive beech forests. In reality, the area contains 33 lakes and marshes, but only twelve are visible from the summit of Mount Okuzure - hence the name. Aoike is known for water of such intense cobalt blue you'd think it was ink-dyed; the exact reason for this color remains scientifically unexplained. With 9 meters of visibility, you can spot beech trunks lying on the bottom from the wooden walkway. In the morning, the water is light blue; in the afternoon, it shifts to deep blue.

A 10-minute walk from Aoike, Wakitsubo Pond is fed by a natural spring whose water is so pure you can drink it directly or sample green tea made from it at the neighboring Juniko-an tea house. It's one of the rare free-access natural water springs in Japan. The 'Nihon Canyon' (Japanese canyon) hiking trail, with its brown and red rock walls contrasting with the greenery, starts from the Juniko entrance - count on 30 minutes one way.

activities.text: Juniko's therapy forests are certified as 'Forest Therapy Road' by the Japanese forest therapy organization - the 2.8 km marsh walk through virgin beech forest immerses you in near-total silence, punctuated by the sound of springs and birdsong. Local guides offer guided hikes into the less accessible parts of Shirakami-Sanchi.

The coast along Fukaura at the edge of the Sea of Japan offers landscapes of rocky capes and basalt cliffs, notably Cape Yukiai (brown-red rocks in blue sea). The Gono Line train route along this coast is one of Tohoku's most beautiful - the Resort Shirakami slows at the best viewpoints. If you're heading to Aomori or Akita by train, this coastal journey is worth the detour.

Note: access to Juniko and Aoike closes from December to March due to snow. Plan your visit from April to November. The site is 15 minutes by bus from JR Juniko Station, served by the Gono Line from Hirosaki (2h30) or Akita (2h). By car from Hirosaki, count approximately 1h30.

visits.text: Transfer Miyajima → Fukuoka: ferry from Miyajima to Miyajimaguchi (10 min), then JR to Hiroshima (30 min), then Nozomi or Sakura Shinkansen to Hakata (about 1h). Allow 2 to 2.5 hours for the entire journey.

Tenjin is Fukuoka's main shopping district—underground shopping arcades (Tenjin Chika), department stores, and boutiques spread across several city blocks. Calmer and more spacious than Tokyo or Osaka shopping districts, it's a good place to wander and buy souvenirs.

Ohori Park, laid out around an artificial lake inspired by Hangzhou's West Lake in China, is Fukuoka's green lung. A 2 km promenade circles the lake—it's locals' favorite jogging circuit. The traditional Japanese garden (paid) on the south shore is an oasis of calm. Just next door, Fukuoka Castle ruins (Maizuru Park) offer a panoramic city view from the former stone terraces of the keep.

Canal City Hakata is a shopping and entertainment complex built around an artificial canal—fountains come alive every 30 minutes with a water and light show. The curved architecture by American Jon Jerde is spectacular. It's also a good eating spot: "Ramen Stadium" on the 5th floor brings together ramen restaurants from across Japan.

Excursion to Dazaifu (30 min by Nishitetsu train from Tenjin). Dazaifu Tenmangū shrine, founded in 905, is dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, deified as Tenjin, the kami of study and literature. Thousands of students come to pray before exams. In front of the shrine, the first plum blossom (ume) supposedly flew from Kyoto to follow its exiled master—the legend of the "flying plum." The access avenue is lined with shops selling umegae-mochi, rice cakes filled with red bean paste and stamped with a plum blossom.

Just behind the shrine, the Kyushu National Museum, designed by Kikkawa Kiyonori, is a massive glass and steel building nestled in the hill. It traces the history of cultural exchanges between Japan, China, and Korea. The architecture alone is worth the trip—the forest reflections on the curved facade are striking.

Nanzōin temple in Sasaguri (20 min by JR from Hakata) houses one of the world's largest reclining bronze Buddhas—41 meters long, 11 meters high, 300 tons. Built in 1995, it's less ancient than its Southeast Asian rivals but its size impresses. The complex is nestled in forest and little frequented by tourists—it's a peaceful place to end your circuit. Take time to visit the 88 small shrines of the miniature Shikoku pilgrimage, scattered in the hills around the temple.

activities.text: In the evening, head to the yatai of Nakasu—these street stalls set up along the Naka River and around Tenjin are a Fukuoka institution. You sit at the counter (6-8 seats maximum per yatai), order a bowl of Hakata ramen (milky pork broth, thin noodles), and chat with the cook and neighbors. Yatai open around 6 pm and close around 1 am. Arrive early to get a seat—they don't take reservations.

Take advantage of your time in Fukuoka to taste different variants of Hakata ramen—creamy tonkotsu (pork bone) broth, ultra-thin noodles you can order extra-firm (kata-men or bari-kata). Most restaurants offer kaedama, an extra portion of noodles to add to your bowl to extend the pleasure.

Before catching the train back, stroll through Dazaifu's historic lanes. This small town was the seat of Kyushu's regional government in the 8th century—it has a tranquil pace and streets lined with traditional houses that contrast with Fukuoka's energy.

Last afternoon in Japan—return to Hakata and Tenjin districts for final shopping. Department store basements (depachika) are the best place to buy boxes of Japanese confections to bring home. Hakata Tōrimon (white bean paste filled cakes) and mentaiko (marinated cod roe) are Fukuoka specialties not to miss.

visits.text: Farm Tomita, founded in 1903, became Japan's most famous lavender farm almost by accident. In the 1960s, imported low-cost lavender threatened Hokkaido producers. Farm Tomita held on, and a photo of its fields published in a JR Railways calendar in the 1970s triggered a tourist wave that never stopped. Today, five lavender varieties succeed each other from late June to early August, peaking in mid-July. The Irodori field, in multicolored horizontal bands on a hillside (purple lavender, red poppies, white gypsophila, yellow daisies), is Furano's most reproduced image. Admission is free.

Flower Land Kamifurano, a few kilometers further north, offers a different perspective: backed by the Tokachi and Ashibetsu peaks, the farm cultivates successive blooms from April to November. Tractor buses tour the fields for visitors wanting an elevated view of the colored bands. The atmosphere is calmer than at Tomita.

activities.text: Arrive at Farm Tomita before 9am in peak season (July): after 10am, parking lots and pathways saturate. Lavender fragrance is more intense in the morning, before heat evaporates essential oils. The shop sells products distilled on-site (essential oil, soaps, candles) - in 1990, the farm's oil won first place at an evaluation conference organized in France.

Climb to the top of Hokusei Hill via the ropeway (paid, ~400 yen) from the neighboring station to overlook the lavender fields with the Tokachi mountains as backdrop. The view from above is that of Hokkaido's iconic photographs. In July, groups of local hikers use the same starting point for alpine trails.

The Furano cheese factory offers free tastings and butter or cheese-making workshops. The region's pastures produce milk of richness recognized throughout Japan. Combine with a tasting at Chateau Furano Winery, set on a hill east of town: light, fruity wines, panoramic view of the valley.

visits.text: Mount Nokogiri (329m) owes its name ("saw-tooth mountain") to the stone quarries that notched its flanks for centuries. You can ascend by ropeway or on foot (trails from 30 to 90 minutes depending on difficulty). At the summit, the "Jigoku Nozoki" (Hell Peek) viewpoint is a rock projection overhanging the void—you lean out over 330 meters of cliff with Tokyo Bay below. Vertigo-inducing.

Nihon-ji Temple, on Mount Nokogiri's flanks, houses a great seated Buddha 31 meters tall carved into rock in 1783. It's Japan's largest stone Buddha. The path leading to it passes 1,553 small arhat statues (rakan) carved into the mountain in the 18th century, each with a different expression.

activities.text: Hike between Mount Nokogiri's different viewpoints—the main trail connects Hell Peek, the great Buddha, and the rakan in about 2 hours. Stairs are steep in places—bring water and good shoes. The view from the summit embraces all of Tokyo Bay, and on clear days you glimpse Mount Fuji.

places.name: Ginzan Onsen

visits.text: Ginzan Onsen ('silver mine hot spring') takes its name from the Nobesawa silver mine that prospered here during the Edo period. Miners discovered a hot spring within the mine itself, and the spa village grew alongside the mining activity. A major flood of the Ginzan River in the 1910s-1920s destroyed most of the inns, which were rebuilt in the Taisho architectural style (1912-1926): bare wood, white plaster, ornate balconies across three or four floors. These buildings have survived a century without being transformed - there are no modern buildings within the village perimeter, giving it a rare architectural homogeneity in Japan. The village is a pedestrian zone; cars stop at the parking lot at the entrance.

The television series Oshin (1983), the most-watched in NHK history, was partly filmed here and made the village famous throughout Japan. Studio Ghibli fans also recognize the atmosphere that reportedly inspired Hayao Miyazaki to create Chihiro's bathhouse in Spirited Away - the Notoya ryokan in particular resembles the film's facade. The Fujiya ryokan was renovated by architect Kengo Kuma in a bold fusion of old and modern that has received much international attention.

activities.text: After sunset, gas lamps light up along the streets and river - this is the village's most photographed hour. In winter, snow accumulates on roofs and bridges, and the lamps' reflections shimmer on the river's black water. Note: since late 2024, a reservation system limits evening access during winter (tickets required from 5pm to 8pm) to control tourist influx.

The walk to Shirogane no Taki waterfall (22 meters high) in Shirogane Park, behind the village, takes 15 minutes on foot. Along the way, you pass the entrance to the old Nobesawa mine (open from May to first snowfall). The Shirogane-yu public bath, also designed by Kengo Kuma, is open to outside visitors.

Ginzan Onsen is in an area of poor cell phone coverage - which also contributes to the timeless atmosphere. Book your ryokan well in advance: the village has only 300 rooms total, and snow periods (December to March) fill up months ahead.

places.name: Hachimantai

visits.text: The Hachimantai Dragon Eye is an ephemeral natural phenomenon that exists only two weeks per year, typically from late May to mid-June. Kagaminuma Pond, near the summit of Mount Hachimantai (1,613 m, on the Akita-Iwate border), gets covered with ice and snow in winter. In spring, the ice melts from the edges, leaving a white disk in the center and a crown of blue water all around. The sky's light reflects in it, creating the illusion of a dragon's eye with an intense blue iris and white pupil. If the wind blows, the ice disk rotates, moving the 'pupil' - on some days, the eye appears alive. From the summit parking lot, count on 15 to 20 minutes' walk to reach the pond.

Don't miss the Aspite Line road, which crosses the Hachimantai massif for 27 km between Kazuno (Akita) and Hachimantai City (Iwate). At the spring opening (mid-April), snowplows create a corridor of snow several meters high on each side of the roadway - Japan's longest. If you pass in June or July, alpine wetlands border the road with wildflowers. Take time to stop at viewpoints - the view of neighboring Mount Iwate is unobstructed.

activities.text: The hike around Hachimantai's summit offers panoramas of volcanic calderas, wetlands, and alpine lakes (Hachminnuma, Gamanuma). The main circuit around the summit takes 2 to 3 hours. In clear weather, you can spot Mount Iwate (2,038 m) in the distance. Alpine plants bloom from July to August.

Tamagawa Onsen, 30 minutes' drive from the summit, is one of Japan's most acidic springs (pH 1.2 - like concentrated vinegar). Its therapeutic virtues are said to be exceptional, and patients with chronic illnesses come to stay for several weeks. The main spring releases 9,000 liters of water per minute, making it Japan's most powerful single spring.

visits.text: From the summit of Mount Hakodate at 334 meters, the city traces an hourglass silhouette of lights between the Tsugaru Strait and the bay - ranked among Japan's three most beautiful night views alongside Nagasaki and Kobe. The ropeway, operating since 1958, reaches the upper station in 3 minutes. Arrive 30 minutes before sunset to watch the city gradually illuminate: the effect peaks during the "blue hour." In summer, beware of frequent fog; in autumn, clear skies provide ideal conditions. Private vehicles are banned on summer evenings from 5pm to 10pm.

Fort Goryokaku, built between 1857 and 1866 following the model of Vauban's bastioned citadels, was Japan's first Western-style fort. Originally designed to repel Russian advances on Hokkaido, it never saw Russo-Japanese conflict. Instead, it became the site of the final battle of the Boshin War in 1869: 2,000 shogunate soldiers took refuge here before surrendering to imperial forces. From the 107-meter Goryokaku Tower (90m observation deck), the five-pointed star shape is striking, especially in spring when 1,660 cherry trees bloom around the moats.

The Motomachi district, at the foot of Mount Hakodate, bears witness to the port's opening to foreigners in 1859 - simultaneously with Yokohama and Nagasaki. Stroll the sloping streets along Hachiman-zaka, lined with Westernized buildings: the old Russian Orthodox church, the former British Consulate, and several missions dating from the Meiji era. The contrast with traditional Japanese architecture is striking and unique in northern Japan.

activities.text: Take the ropeway to the summit in 3 minutes from Sanroku Station, accessible in 10 minutes on foot from the Jujigai tram stop. Ascend 30 minutes before sunset - that's when the city begins to sparkle and crowds have already gathered. The panoramic restaurant at the summit accepts highly sought-after reservations for tables on the night-view side.

Head down to Asaichi morning market from 5am, steps from JR Hakodate Station. It's one of Hokkaido's liveliest fish markets: vendors offer tastings of shrimp, sea urchin, and king crab directly from their stalls. The local specialty is kaisendon - a rice bowl topped with ultra-fresh seafood that you choose yourself from the displayed products.

In the evening, stroll along the red-brick Kanemori warehouses dating from 1909, converted into shops and restaurants at the harbor's edge. Their reflections in the harbor water at night create one of Hakodate's iconic images. If you're looking for local beer to enjoy on the quays, Hakodate Beer brews in one of the old warehouses.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: The pampas grass fields (susuki) of Sengokuhara, between Hakone and Gotemba, stretch across a vast plain. From late September to early November, silvery plumes sway in the breeze and gradually change color—from silvery green to deep gold as autumn advances. At sunset, raking light illuminates the plumes by transparency—that's when you need to be there. A short trail crosses the field.

Take advantage of your time in Hakone to revisit Owakudani Valley and its volcanic fumaroles (see Day 6 of the classic Japan itinerary), or for a cruise on Lake Ashi with views of Fuji on clear days. The torii gate of Hakone-jinja Shrine planted in the lake is one of the region's most famous photo spots.

activities.text: End the day in a Hakone onsen—the hot spring resort is one of the most renowned in Kanto. Many establishments offer outdoor baths (rotenburo) with views of mountains or valley. Some are open to drop-in visitors for a few hours.

visits.text: The Genbikei Gorge stretches for 2 km along the Iwai River at Ichinoseki. Its rock formations sculpted by erosion were designated a National Natural Monument in 1927. Lord Date Masamune himself, governor of Tohoku in the early 17th century, praised this gorge's beauty as comparable to Japan's three most beautiful views. The river waters flow in a striking turquoise blue between gray-white rock cliffs, dotted with strangely shaped formations that locals have given poetic names.

Hiraizumi, 20 minutes by bus from Ichinoseki, has been inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2011 for its 12th-century Buddhist temples. Chusonji Temple - founded in 850 and rebuilt in the 12th century by the Fujiwara clan - houses the Konjikido, a pavilion entirely covered in gold leaf containing the tombs of four Fujiwara lords. Motsuji Temple, 1 km away, preserves the finest Heian period (794-1185) garden still existing in Japan.

activities.text: The great attraction of Genbikei isn't visual but gastronomic: the 'flying' Kakko Dango. Since 1878, the Kakkôya shop has delivered its mochi skewers (sesame, anko, mitarashi) to visitors on the other side of the gorge via a basket on a cable - a homemade zipline that crosses the gorge at high speed, all without spilling the green tea that accompanies it. Put your money in the basket, strike the wooden board to signal your order, and watch the delivery fly back across the canyon.

Walk the Genbikei trails along the river from Tenku Bridge to the various viewpoints. Goranba Bridge offers a plunging view of the rock formations and rapids below. In autumn, the contrast between the pale rocks and flaming foliage is particularly spectacular.

days_plan.itin_title: Tohoku Lakes & Volcanoes Circuit

visits.text: Hirosaki Castle Park is universally recognized as one of Japan's three most beautiful cherry blossom viewing sites. Its 2,600 cherry trees of more than 50 varieties - some over 300 years old - bloom from mid-April to early May, 2 to 3 weeks after Tokyo. The distinctive feature of these trees: gardeners apply apple tree pruning techniques (Aomori is Japan's top apple-producing region), giving the cherry trees more spreading branches and denser blooms than elsewhere in the archipelago. Over 2 million visitors come each year during the festival.

Hirosaki Castle dates from 1611 (built by Nobuhira Tsugaru); lightning destroyed it in 1627, and reconstruction didn't begin until 1810 - the current tower is one of twelve original castle towers still standing in all of Japan, and one of the smallest. It's classified as an Important Cultural Property. During the cherry blossom festival, you can see the rafts of petals floating in the outer moats at the end of bloom - a fragile pink spectacle that the Japanese call hanaikada.

activities.text: The cherry tree tunnel along the west moats is Hirosaki's most photographed image: the branches meet above the path and form a complete pink canopy for a few days. Arrive before 8am to avoid crowds or after 6pm to see the nighttime illumination (until 10pm during the festival) - the vision of illuminated cherry trees in darkness with the moats as mirror is sumptuous.

The Neputamura Museum, in the park's northern section, is dedicated to Tsugaru arts - the Hirosaki region. You can see demonstrations of Tsugaru shamisen (northern electric fiddle) and large lanterns from the Neputa Festival (Hirosaki's counterpart to Aomori's Nebuta Festival, in August). Pottery and lacquer workshops are also offered.

Hirosaki has a historic downtown with Meiji and Taisho period buildings, notably the Catholic cathedral (1910), the old public library, and Higashi-Kogyo school. The Nakamachi district concentrates merchant and minor samurai residences. Outside the festival, Hirosaki is also famous for its apples - the local apple pie is a must-try.

days_plan.itin_title: Tohoku Lakes & Volcanoes Circuit

visits.text: Pay respects at the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park, a profound space dedicated to the victims of the world's first atomic bombing and the pursuit of lasting peace. The park encompasses memorials, monuments, and the haunting A-Bomb Dome, preserved as it stood after the explosion. This UNESCO World Heritage site serves as both a memorial and a powerful message for nuclear disarmament.

Visit the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum, whose exhibits present the reality of the atomic bombing through survivors' belongings, photographs, and personal testimonies. The museum chronicles events before, during, and after August 6, 1945, creating a deeply moving educational experience. Recent renovations have enhanced the focus on individual human stories.

Stand before the Atomic Bomb Dome (Genbaku Dome), the skeletal remains of the Hiroshima Prefectural Industrial Promotion Hall that survived nearly directly beneath the explosion. This UNESCO World Heritage structure serves as a stark reminder of the bomb's destructive power and humanity's capacity for both destruction and resilience. The dome has become an international symbol of peace.

activities.text: Explore Hiroshima Castle, faithfully reconstructed after its complete destruction in the atomic bombing, now housing a museum dedicated to the city's pre-war history and samurai culture. The castle, originally built in 1589, offers views over the city from its observation deck and displays armor, swords, and historical artifacts. The surrounding moat and grounds provide pleasant walking paths.

Discover the serene beauty of Shukkei-en Garden, a traditional Japanese stroll garden dating to 1620, designed to represent famous landscapes in miniature. The garden features valleys, mountains, forests, and beaches compressed into a single scenic pond garden. Though destroyed in 1945, the garden was carefully restored to its original Edo-period design.

Experience Hiroshima's vibrant modern culture and famous okonomiyaki, the savory pancakes layered with noodles, cabbage, pork, and special sauce that differ distinctly from Osaka style. The city has rebuilt into a thriving metropolis that celebrates life while never forgetting its past. The evening atmosphere along the Hon-dori shopping arcade showcases the city's resilient spirit.

visits.text: Hitachi Seaside Park stretches across 190 hectares along the Pacific coast, 1.5 hours by train from Tokyo (JR Joban Line from Ueno to Katsuta, then bus). The park's star attraction is Miharashi Hill—from mid-April to early May, 4.5 million nemophila (translucent baby-blue flowers) blanket its 3.5-hectare slopes. The gradient between flower blue, sky blue, and ocean blue is breathtaking. Arrive before 8:30am if you're visiting during Golden Week (April 29–May 5)—after 10am, crowds get dense. Spring brings nemophila (April-May). Autumn transforms the hill with crimson kochia (October). Summer blooms with sunflowers.

Beyond Miharashi Hill, the park hosts a million daffodils (late March to mid-April), 170 tulip varieties, and a 100-meter Ferris wheel offering aerial views of the grounds. Cycling paths crisscross the park—if time permits, rent a bike to reach the more distant zones. Before leaving, try the nemophila ice cream (turquoise blue, ramune flavor) sold at the food court stalls at the hill's base.

activities.text: After the park, detour to Nakaminato fishing port, 10 minutes by car. The fish market here is outstanding—you can lunch on ultra-fresh sashimi served directly by fishermen. It's one of the biggest Pacific fishing ports in the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey

places.name: Hokuryu

visits.text: Hokuryu counts only 1,575 inhabitants - fewer than the number of visitors on certain summer weekends. Yet the town hosts Japan's largest sunflower fields: 2 million flowers covering 23 hectares of gentle hills. The Himawari no Sato festival has existed since 1979, initially cultivated by local farmers to valorize difficult land. The World Sunflower Corner displays 24 distinct varieties of sunflowers, from giants to dwarfs, from golden yellow to burgundy. Blooming extends from late July to mid-August.

The elevated observation point, accessible from the fields, offers a bird's-eye view of the yellow sea stretching to the horizon. In clear weather, the rolling hills evoke the Pre-Alps in miniature. Field admission is free; only the giant sunflower maze and bike rentals are paid.

activities.text: Get lost in the giant sunflower maze, whose pathways change configuration each year. The challenge can take 20 to 45 minutes depending on your sense of direction. Late morning, the flowers are still fresh and the sun hasn't yet bent the heads - it's the best time for photography.

At the Himawari information center, taste sunflower ice cream flavored with seed oil, and local roasted seeds. Tourist tractors traverse the fields for those who don't want to walk. Outside sunflower season (before mid-July or after mid-August), the fields are green and uninteresting - check dates before making the detour.

Evening thermal bath at Sunflower Park Hokuryu Onsen, steps from the fields. This non-touristy public bath lets you relax after a day on the road and mingle with residents of this deep Hokkaido countryside.

visits.text: Kabira Bay is ranked among Japan's 100 Most Beautiful Views—the water's intense turquoise, dubbed "Kabira Blue," surrounds small islets covered in tropical vegetation. Swimming is prohibited due to currents, but glass-bottom boats let you admire corals and tropical fish without getting wet. This is also where Okinawa's black pearls are cultivated.

Banna Forest Park covers a central Ishigaki hill with several observation platforms. The Emerald Sea Observatory offers the best view of the sea and Yaeyama archipelago islands. Trails wind through subtropical forest where butterflies unique to the region flutter.
Ishigaki Limestone Cave, 200,000 years old, is Japan's southernmost tourist cave. The 660-meter route passes through illuminated halls with stalactites—one resembles the character Totoro.

activities.text: Morning flight from Naha to Ishigaki—about 1 hour. Arrive at Naha Airport at least 1 hour before departure. From Ishigaki Airport, rent a car: the island is compact, 30 minutes is enough to reach Kabira Bay.
Board a glass-bottom boat in Kabira Bay to observe corals and tropical fish—departures are frequent, allow about 30 minutes.
Taste Ishigaki beef, considered one of Japan's finest wagyu. Downtown restaurants serve it as yakiniku or sushi.

days_plan.itin_title: Okinawa and Ryukyu Islands Journey: Beaches and Culture

visits.text: Umikaji Terrace, on Senaga Island (1.8 km circumference), is a complex opened in 2015 whose white terraced buildings cascade toward the sea—you'd think you were in Santorini. Over 40 shops and restaurants. The island is 15 minutes from the airport. Go in late afternoon: the sunset over the Kerama Islands is spectacular, and planes land in silhouette.

The Himeyuri Memorial tells the heartbreaking story of 222 high school girls and 18 teachers mobilized as auxiliary nurses during the Battle of Okinawa in 1945. Abandoned on the battlefield on June 18, 136 of them perished. The museum, built in 1989 by survivors and renovated in 2021, features video testimonies and a recreation of an underground surgical shelter. Plan at least an hour.

Ryukyu Glass Village in Itoman showcases the art of blown glass that emerged after the war, when artisans recycled bottles left behind by American soldiers. The vivid colors—ocean blue, emerald green—are the signature of this craft. You can watch glassblowers at their 1,300°C furnaces.

activities.text: Join a glassblowing workshop—in 15 minutes, you'll create your own glass piece under an artisan's guidance. The piece will be shipped to you after cooling.
Enjoy the sunset from Umikaji Terrace. In the evening, a projection mapping show plays every 30 minutes starting at 7:30 PM.
Relax at Senaga Island's natural hot springs with ocean views—rare in Okinawa. Perfect after the emotional visit to the Himeyuri Memorial.

visits.text: Ryusendo Cave is one of Japan's three great limestone caverns - along with Akiyoshido (Yamaguchi) and Ryugado (Kochi) - designated a national natural monument since 1938. The total explored length exceeds 4,088 meters (an estimate of 5,000 m for the whole), of which 700 meters are open to the public. Ryusendo's distinctive feature is the presence of four underground lakes fed by artesian springs: the third reaches 98 meters deep and holds the most transparent waters ever measured in a cave anywhere in the world - in winter, when rains no longer disturb the sediments, visibility is total to the bottom.

The waters of Ryusendo's underground lakes have a tint locally called 'dragon blue' - a shade of intense deep blue caused by light reflection on minerals suspended in exceptionally pure water. LED lighting installed in 2011 highlights the reliefs and stalactites. The temperature inside the cave is a constant 10°C year-round - bring a jacket, even in midsummer.

activities.text: The adjacent Ryusen Shindo cave (discovered in 1967), separated by the Shizu River, houses the 'world's first natural cave science museum' according to its managers. Pottery discovered during the 1967 excavations and explanations of karst geology are exhibited there. Ryusendo's bottled water is sold as premium spring water throughout Japan - and it's the same water that comes from Iwaizumi residents' taps.

Thirty minutes north of Iwaizumi lies the Kitayamazaki Coast: 200-meter-high basalt cliffs plunge directly into the Pacific Ocean for several kilometers. Boats offer cruises at the foot of the cliffs from Omoto Port in summer. The Sanriku Coast, of which Iwaizumi is part, is designated a national park.

places.name: Kakunodate

visits.text: The Dakigaeri Gorge - whose name means 'embrace to pass through' in reference to the need to hold onto each other on the historically narrow path - is one of Akita's most beautiful nature reserves. The Tamagawa River flows in an almost surreal cobalt blue between limestone rock formations covered with virgin forest. A 2-hour round-trip trail starts from Dakigaeri Shrine and leads to Mikaeri no Taki waterfall (30 meters high, whose name means 'turn back to look at it again'). Along the way, you cross two suspension bridges including Kami no Iwabashi, Akita's oldest bridge (built in 1926).

Kakunodate, nicknamed the 'Little Kyoto of Tohoku,' was founded in 1620 as a castle town of the Satake clan. Its black-lacquered wooden samurai houses, surrounded by high walls and weeping cherries, have remained virtually unchanged since the Edo period. Six residences are open to the public, including the largest, Aoyagi House, which houses a true museum complex with collections of armor, swords, and historical documents. Ishiguro House is inhabited by direct descendants of the samurai family - the visit takes on a particular human dimension.

activities.text: Kabazaiku - the art of covering wood and objects with cherry bark - has been Kakunodate's artisan specialty since the 18th century, introduced by a local samurai. The Denshokan Museum displays tea boxes, furniture, and jewelry made with this technique, and you can observe artisans at work. Shops on the main street offer pieces ranging from a few thousand to several hundred thousand yen.

In spring (late April to early May), more than 162 weeping cherry trees designated national natural monuments bloom in the samurai quarter, creating a spectacular contrast between their pink flowers and the black walls of the residences. Over one million visitors come each year for this bloom. Off-season, the district remains magnificent with its winter snow or autumn leaves.

Akita's culinary specialties are must-tries: kiritanpo (grilled rice rolls on cedar skewers, served in soup), inaniwa udon noodles (thin and silky, handmade for 160 years), and hinai jidori chicken, one of Japan's three best chicken breeds.

visits.text: Kamakura's Great Buddha (Daibutsu), at Kōtoku-in Temple, measures 11.3 meters tall (13.35m with base) and weighs about 121 tons of bronze. Built starting in 1252, it was originally gilded and sheltered in a wooden temple—but a tsunami in 1498 swept away the building, leaving the Buddha in the open air ever since. You can enter inside the hollow statue. It's Japan's second-largest seated bronze Buddha, after the one in Nara. Allow 30-45 minutes for the visit.

A few minutes' walk away, Hase-dera Temple, founded in the 8th century, perches on a hillside with views over Sagami Bay. Its eleven-headed Kannon statue (9.18m) is one of Japan's largest wooden statues. The terraced gardens, lotus ponds, and cave with thousands of small Benten statues are enchanting. Climb to the viewpoint at the temple's top—the view over sea and city is Kamakura's finest.

Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū Shrine, founded in 1063 by Minamoto no Yoriyoshi, is Kamakura's spiritual heart. A long avenue bordered by cherry trees (Dankazura) leads from the sea to the shrine—in April, it's a flower tunnel. From the top of the stairs, views plunge down the entire avenue to the ocean.

If you have time, push on to Hōkoku-ji Temple (bamboo temple), founded in 1334. Its bamboo forest, more intimate than Arashiyama's in Kyoto, can be toured in a few minutes. Sit in the tea house to enjoy matcha facing the bamboo—it's one of the most zen moments of the entire itinerary.

activities.text: Kamakura is ideally explored on foot or by renting a bicycle. The Daibutsu hiking trail (about 3km, 1h30) connects Kita-Kamakura Station to the Great Buddha, passing through Jōchi-ji Temple and Zeniarai Benzaiten Shrine (a shrine in a cave where visitors wash their coins to make them multiply). It's a beautiful route to conclude your Kanto circuit.

days_plan.itin_title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey

visits.text: The Taushubetsu Bridge is a concrete arch bridge 130 meters long, 10 meters high with 11 arches, built in 1937 to allow the former Shihoro railway line to cross the river of the same name. In 1955, construction of the Nukabira Dam flooded the line and bridge. Since then, the bridge emerges and disappears under water according to reservoir level - hence its nickname "phantom bridge." It's generally visible from January to August, its arches recalling a Roman aqueduct. The forest road leading there is closed to the public: access is exclusively by guided tour organized by the local guide center (reservation mandatory). In winter, snowshoe hikes on the frozen lake allow approaching it.

Mikuni Pass, at 1,139 meters altitude, is Hokkaido's highest road pass. It marks the border between Kamikawa and Tokachi subprefectures and offers views of dense forest of Ezo spruce and Sakhalin fir. Autumn foliage is spectacular from late September to early October. Several developed lookouts line the road.

activities.text: Book the Taushubetsu Bridge guided tour in advance through the Higashi-taisetsu Nature Guide Center. Tours generally depart in the morning and last 2 to 3 hours. In summer (July), the bridge reflects in the lake's calm waters on windless mornings - it's the image most sought by photographers. The bridge deteriorates year by year: each freeze-thaw cycle erodes the concrete, portions have collapsed. Visit it while it still stands.

Lake Nukabira, Hokkaido's second-largest artificial lake, is bordered by Nukabira Onsen hot spring resort. In winter, the frozen lake hosts ice smelt fishing and snowshoe walks. Summer, trails follow the shores through the forest. Horoka Onsen hot spring, nearby, gushes in open nature.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: Depart from Kansai International Airport, an architectural marvel built on a man-made island in Osaka Bay, designed by renowned architect Renzo Piano. The airport's elegant terminal, shaped like an aircraft wing, offers excellent shopping and dining facilities for last-minute souvenirs and Japanese cuisine. Arrive early to explore the observation deck for final views of the bay and surrounding mountains before bidding farewell to Japan.

activities.text: Browse the extensive duty-free shopping areas offering Japanese crafts, electronics, cosmetics, and regional food specialties. The airport provides an excellent opportunity to pick up last-minute gifts including matcha products, sake, and traditional sweets. Several restaurants serve quality Japanese dishes for one final taste of the country's cuisine.

visits.text: Kasama Azalea Park (Kasama Tsutsuji Kōen), perched at 143 meters altitude, explodes with color from mid-April to mid-May during the azalea festival. About 8,500 azaleas of 25 varieties blanket the hillside—from vivid crimson to pale pink to pure white. From the summit, views sweep over the rolling Ibaraki countryside. Arrive late afternoon for golden light on the flowers—and stay for sunset, which is superb from the hilltop viewpoint.

Kasama is also a pottery town—Kasama-yaki ceramics have been produced since the 18th century. Tour the artisan quarter where you can watch potters at work and purchase pieces directly. Kasama Inari Shrine, one of Japan's three greatest Inari shrines, makes a good complement—its vermilion torii gates in the forest echo Fushimi Inari in miniature.

activities.text: If pottery appeals to you, workshops offer beginner sessions on the potter's wheel (allow 1 to 1.5 hours). You shape your piece and it's mailed to you after firing—a unique souvenir to bring home from Japan.

visits.text: The Isumi Railway, in the Bōsō Peninsula, crosses bright yellow rapeseed fields (nanohana) from March to April. Retro diesel trains with one or two cars roll through the midst of flowers—it's one of the region's most prized photo spots. The Ishigami rapeseed field, along the tracks, is the most photogenic. Position yourself trackside 10 minutes before the train passes for the perfect shot: yellow flowers in foreground, vintage red train, blue sky.

Egawa Coast in Kisarazu is nicknamed "the Uyuni Salt Flat of Kanto." At high tide, when water covers the sandbanks, fishing poles (lined up in the sea) reflect on the surface like a giant mirror. Best moment: 1 to 2 hours before low tide, in calm weather. At sunset, the effect is striking—the orange sky doubles in the water.

activities.text: If you're at Egawa Coast in late afternoon, wait for sunset—the light conditions transform the beach into an almost surreal landscape. Bring rubber boots if you want to walk on the still-wet sandbanks.

visits.text: Kushiro Shitsugen National Park covers 268 km² and constitutes Japan's largest non-degraded wetland. Listed under the Ramsar Convention in 1980 - Japan's first site - and established as a national park in 1987, this expanse of reeds and sinuous rivers shelters more than 700 plant species and 1,300 animal species. The Japanese crane (tancho-zuru), considered extinct in the early 20th century, was rediscovered here in 1926 at about twenty individuals. Thanks to conservation efforts, their population now exceeds 1,700 individuals. This crane - white and black with a bright red crown, 150 cm tall and up to 250 cm wingspan - is classified as a Special Natural Treasure of Japan.

Hosooka Observatory overlooks the marsh from a forested ridge. A boardwalk and developed lookouts allow 360° views of the wetlands' green-russet carpet, the meandering Kushiro River, and mountains on the horizon. Wings of Steller's sea eagles, silhouettes of sika deer, and sometimes cranes are distinguishable in the vegetation.

activities.text: Board the Norokko tourist train (April to early October) from Kushiro Station to Toro, running along the Kushiro River for 45 minutes through the marsh. From the windows, you regularly spot sika deer, Ezo red foxes, and sometimes red-crowned cranes. The train slows at the most picturesque spots. Seats on the river side (left heading to Toro) offer the best views.

In winter (November to March), the crane feeding stations at Tsurui and Akan gather large groups of birds. The Akan International Crane Center, open year-round, overlooks the feeding meadows - by far the best configuration for photographing cranes in their spectacular courtship dances without disturbing them.

Canoe down the Kushiro River through the marsh's heart: local providers offer half-day excursions from April to October. The river is slow and calm, perfect for silent navigation that doesn't disturb wildlife. It's the most immersive way to approach the wetlands.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: Walk through the magical tunnel of 10,000 vermillion torii gates at Fushimi Inari Taisha, Japan's most iconic Shinto shrine dedicated to the god of rice and prosperity. The winding path ascends Mount Inari through dense forests, with gates donated by businesses hoping for success. The full hike to the summit takes about two hours and rewards with serene forest views.

Marvel at the UNESCO-listed Kiyomizu-dera Temple, whose massive wooden terrace juts out over the hillside supported by 139 pillars without a single nail. This 8th-century temple offers sweeping views over Kyoto's forested hills and the city below, particularly breathtaking during cherry blossom and autumn foliage seasons. The temple's sacred Otowa Waterfall is believed to grant wishes.

Wander the enchanting historic streets of Sannenzaka and Ninenzaka, traditional stone-paved lanes lined with beautifully preserved wooden machiya houses. These atmospheric slopes leading to Kiyomizu-dera are filled with tea houses, pottery shops, and traditional sweet stores. The area perfectly captures the essence of old Kyoto and offers countless photo opportunities.

Wander through the otherworldly Arashiyama Bamboo Grove, where towering green stalks create a natural cathedral that filters sunlight into an ethereal glow. The rustling of bamboo in the breeze creates a sound so distinctive it's been designated a cultural treasure by the Japanese government. Early morning visits offer the most magical atmosphere before crowds arrive.

Explore Tenryu-ji Temple, a UNESCO World Heritage site featuring one of Japan's finest examples of borrowed scenery garden design, with the Arashiyama mountains forming a spectacular backdrop. Founded in 1339, this Zen temple's garden was designed by the famous landscape architect Muso Soseki and has remained virtually unchanged for 700 years. The reflection of the hills in the pond creates a living painting.

Visit the stunning Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion), a Zen temple whose top two floors are completely covered in gold leaf, creating a dazzling reflection in the surrounding mirror pond. Originally built as a retirement villa for a shogun in 1397, the pavilion represents the harmony of heaven and earth in Buddhist cosmology. The meticulously maintained gardens showcase Japanese landscape perfection.

Hike through the mystical mountain trails connecting Kurama and Kibune, two ancient villages north of Kyoto steeped in spiritual tradition. The forested path passes through giant cedar trees, moss-covered stones, and the powerful Kurama-dera Temple, believed to be home to supernatural tengu spirits. This sacred mountain has been a training ground for mountain ascetics for over 1,200 years.

Discover Kurama-dera Temple, dramatically perched on the mountainside and accessed by a scenic cable car or forest hiking trail. This mystical temple is associated with the legendary warrior Yoshitsune, who trained here as a boy, and is believed to possess powerful spiritual energy. The temple's main hall offers commanding views across the valley.

Visit the ethereal Kifune Shrine, dedicated to the god of water and nestled in a forested valley famous for its cool summer temperatures and mystical atmosphere. Red lanterns line the stone steps leading to the shrine, creating a particularly magical scene at dusk or in light rain. The shrine is popular for love fortune-telling using special water divination papers.

Stand before the awe-inspiring Daibutsu at Todai-ji Temple in Nara, the world's largest bronze Buddha statue housed within the world's largest wooden building. This 15-meter seated Buddha, cast in 752 AD, required the labor of 2.6 million people and nearly bankrupted the Japanese government. The massive wooden hall, despite being rebuilt at only two-thirds its original size, remains an engineering marvel.

Wander through Nara Park, where over 1,000 sacred deer roam freely among ancient temples and shrines, bowing to visitors in exchange for special deer crackers. These gentle animals have been protected for centuries as divine messengers of the Shinto gods. The park encompasses many of Nara's UNESCO World Heritage sites within its vast green spaces.

Explore the ancient Kasuga Taisha Shrine, famous for its hundreds of bronze and stone lanterns donated by worshippers over the centuries. The vermilion structures set against the primeval forest create an atmosphere of timeless spirituality. Twice yearly, during the Mantoro Festival, all 3,000 lanterns are lit, transforming the shrine into a magical scene.

activities.text: Explore the mysterious Gion district, Kyoto's most famous geisha quarter where traditional wooden machiya houses line narrow streets and geiko and maiko still practice their art. The Hanami-koji street offers the best chance of spotting these elegant entertainers hurrying to evening appointments. Traditional ochaya (tea houses) preserve centuries-old entertainment traditions.

Browse the colorful stalls of Nishiki Market, known as 'Kyoto's Kitchen,' stretching over five blocks with vendors selling fresh seafood, pickles, tofu, and seasonal delicacies. This 400-year-old market offers tastings of local specialties like yuba (tofu skin), Japanese sweets, and matcha treats. The market provides an authentic glimpse into Kyoto's culinary heritage.

Discover the hidden temples along the route from Fushimi Inari, including the peaceful Komyo-in with its contemplative zen garden and the historic Sennyu-ji, final resting place of many Japanese emperors. These lesser-visited temples offer intimate spiritual experiences away from the crowds, with beautiful gardens and ancient architecture.

Cross the iconic Togetsukyo Bridge spanning the Katsura River, a symbol of Arashiyama since the Heian period offering sweeping views of the forested mountains and traditional boats. The bridge is particularly scenic when autumn colors paint the surrounding hills or during cherry blossom season. Rent a traditional rowboat to experience the river from a different perspective.

Tour the exquisite Okochi-Sanso Villa, the former home of silent film star Okochi Denjiro, featuring stunning stroll gardens with panoramic views of Kyoto and the surrounding mountains. This hidden gem at the end of the bamboo grove offers peaceful garden paths, traditional tea houses, and spectacular vistas. Admission includes matcha tea and sweets in a traditional setting.

Contemplate the profound simplicity of Ryoan-ji's rock garden, Japan's most famous Zen garden featuring 15 carefully arranged stones on raked white gravel. This minimalist masterpiece has puzzled and inspired visitors for over 500 years, with its meaning remaining deliberately ambiguous. The surrounding temple grounds include a beautiful pond garden and historic structures.

Walk the serene Philosopher's Path, a two-kilometer stone walkway following a cherry tree-lined canal connecting several of Kyoto's most beautiful temples. Named after the philosopher Nishida Kitaro who practiced meditation while walking here, the path invites contemplation and peaceful strolling. Charming cafés, boutiques, and galleries dot the route.

Explore Ginkaku-ji (Silver Pavilion), a Zen temple famous for its beautiful gardens and sand sculpture representing Mount Fuji and the sea, rather than any silver covering. The modest aesthetic of this 15th-century retreat reflects the refined wabi-sabi philosophy of finding beauty in imperfection. The moss garden and borrowed scenery design are considered artistic masterpieces.

Discover the hidden Honen-in Temple, a tranquil Zen retreat along the Philosopher's Path featuring a thatched gate, raked sand gardens, and moss-covered grounds. This intimate temple hosts seasonal art exhibitions and maintains an atmosphere of profound peace rarely found even in Kyoto. The sculptured sand mounds at the entrance are works of ephemeral art.

Visit the Nara National Museum to view one of Japan's finest collections of Buddhist art and treasures, including sacred statues and religious artifacts spanning 1,300 years. The museum's galleries provide essential context for understanding the Buddhist temples scattered throughout the ancient capital. Special exhibitions during the Shoso-in treasure season reveal imperial treasures rarely displayed.

Stroll through Naramachi, the traditional merchant district of Nara, where narrow lanes wind between beautifully preserved machiya townhouses converted into craft shops, galleries, and cafés. This atmospheric neighborhood offers glimpses into Nara's Edo-period prosperity and features unique local crafts including Nara-sarashi textiles and traditional calligraphy brushes.

Find peace in the exquisite Isuien Garden, a masterpiece of Japanese landscape design using 'borrowed scenery' to incorporate Todai-ji Temple and Mount Wakakusa into its composition. This Meiji-era garden features two distinct sections representing different design philosophies, connected by traditional tea houses. The garden offers one of Nara's most photographed views.

visits.text: Lake Ōnuma, in the caldera of Mount Akagi (1,828m), is the mountain's largest caldera lake. In winter (January-February), when the lake freezes and there's little snow, a rare phenomenon occurs: methane bubbles rising from the bottom remain trapped in transparent ice, creating a spectacle of "ice bubbles" (kōri no awa)—it looks like bubbles frozen in glass. It's one of the rare places in Kanto where this phenomenon is observable. Check weather conditions before going—it requires intense cold and little snow.

In any season, Mount Akagi offers beautiful hikes around the lake and toward surrounding peaks. In spring, wild azaleas color the slopes; in autumn, maples reflect in the lake. The trail circling Lake Ōnuma (about 1 hour) is accessible to everyone.

activities.text: Descend to Maebashi for lunch. The city is known for its tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlets) and for Maebashi Municipal Rose Garden, which brings together over 600 rose varieties—free and lovely in spring and early summer.

visits.text: Matsushima - 'pine islands' - is one of Japan's three most beautiful views (Nihon Sankei), designated by Confucian scholar Hayashi Gahō (1618-1680) alongside Amanohashidate and Itsukushima. The bay contains approximately 260 islets covered with pines twisted by sea winds, each one the emerging summit of an ancient valley flooded by tectonic movements. Poet Matsuo Bashô (1644-1694) was so overwhelmed he reportedly couldn't find words to compose a haiku - an admission of defeat before beauty. The 2011 tsunami reached the bay, but the islets acted as natural breakwaters, significantly reducing the damage.

Zuiganji Temple is one of Tohoku's most important Zen temples, founded in the 9th century and rebuilt in 1609 by Lord Date Masamune to serve as his clan's family temple. Its richly painted gold interior decoration is one of the finest from the Edo period. Godaido Hall - founded in 828, rebuilt in 1604 - sits on a rock facing the pier: it's one of Matsushima's most photographed visual symbols. The Kanrantei tea house, a gift from Toyotomi Hideyoshi to Date Masamune, offers a majestic view of the bay from the shore.

Since the late Edo period, four panoramic viewpoints of the bay have been selected and named the 'four great views of Matsushima' (Shidaikan): Ôtakamori (105 m), Tomiyama, Tamonzan, and Ogitani. Reaching Ôtakamori by car or on foot from Higashi-Matsushima lets you see the entire bay at once - no buildings obstruct the view from the summit. Allow an extra half hour for this detour.

activities.text: The bay cruise is the best way to see the 260 islets up close. Several options depart from the pier: a 50-minute loop around the bay, or a longer cruise to Shiogama (neighboring town with an exceptional seafood market). Bilingual audio commentary identifies the most remarkable islands. If you have time, combine the cruise with the ferry return from Shiogama - you avoid backtracking by car.

Fukuura-jima Island is connected to the mainland by a 252-meter red-lacquered bridge - one of the bay's visual landmarks. The island itself is a wild botanical garden with 300 plant varieties. An hour's walk is enough to circle it, with splendid views of the other islets' rock formations.

Matsushima is also famous for its oysters: the bay is a major producer. Don't leave without tasting some fresh oysters grilled on the barbecue or in kaki nabe (oyster soup) at one of the waterfront restaurants. The best time for oysters is the Kaki Matsuri (Oyster Festival) every first Saturday in February, but they're available year-round.

visits.text: The Miharu Takizakura is simply one of Japan's three greatest cherry trees - designated a national monument as early as 1922, making it the first tree in the archipelago to receive this honor. Standing 12 meters tall with branches extending 20 meters in every direction, it creates the impression of a waterfall of pink petals cascading toward the ground. The tree has lived for over 1,000 years and its trunk measures 9.5 meters in circumference. Arrive early in the morning during peak bloom (mid to late April): the road leading to the site gets congested quickly, and a special shuttle runs from Miharu Station to avoid the traffic jams.

From the top of the small hill that serves as the tree's pedestal, the sweeping view over the blossoming tree and the rice paddies of the plain is magnificent. Then descend to ground level for the upward perspective: the branches tower above you and petals fall like pink snow. Note that the tree has been surrounded by a protective fence since the heavy snowfalls of 2005-2006 broke several of its branches - locals hand-cleared the snow to save it. The 2011 earthquake fortunately caused no damage, and the tree has since become a symbol of resilience for the entire Fukushima region.

activities.text: The nighttime illumination of the Takizakura is something special: the tree projects its reflections into the darkness and the petals glow under the spotlights. If you're here during bloom season, don't miss the evening version - the site stays open after dark during peak season and the perspective differs radically from the daytime visit.

The town of Miharu - whose name means 'three springs' - also boasts 10,000 cherry trees scattered throughout the surrounding countryside, including 70 century-old weeping specimens. If you've rented a car, explore the country lanes connecting these trees to the Takizakura: it's one of the best hanami circuits away from the crowds.

At the stand near the site entrance, sample local sakura-flavored specialties: ice creams, teas, and cherry blossom cakes. The site entrance fee (approximately 300 yen during bloom season) directly funds the tree's maintenance and protection.

visits.text: The Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum, renovated in 2019, traces with sobriety and unflinching honesty the events of August 6, 1945, when the atomic bomb destroyed 90% of the city in an instant. Victims' personal effects, testimonies, and models are deeply moving. Allow at least 1.5 hours—and prepare yourself emotionally.

The Genbaku Dome (A-Bomb Dome), the only building left standing near the hypocenter, has been preserved as-is as a memorial. It's the former Prefectural Industrial Promotion Hall, designed by Czech architect Jan Letzel and completed in 1915. UNESCO-listed in 1996. The surrounding Peace Memorial Park, with its cenotaph, peace flame, and peace bell, should be visited in silence—it's a place of reflection.

Itsukushima Shrine, founded in 593 and rebuilt in its current form in 1168 by Taira no Kiyomori, seems to float on water at high tide. Its great 16-meter vermilion torii, planted in the sea 200 meters from shore, is one of Japan's symbols. At low tide, you can walk to the torii's base—check tide times before planning your visit. The entire shrine is UNESCO-listed.

Daishō-in temple, on the slopes of Mount Misen, is often overshadowed by Itsukushima but well worth the detour. It's the island's main Buddhist temple—its staircases lined with hundreds of copper prayer wheels, its statues covered with knitted caps, and its sea view are enchanting. Less crowded than the shrine, it offers a more intimate atmosphere.

activities.text: After this intense visit, rent a bike to explore central Hiroshima, rebuilt around its seven rivers. The city has a pleasant rhythm, calmer than the major metropolises. Detour through Hondōri district to taste Hiroshima okonomiyaki—different from Osaka's: here the layers (noodles, cabbage, egg, sauce) are stacked instead of mixed.

Ascending Mount Misen (535 m), Miyajima's highest point, can be done on foot (about 1.5 hours of sustained climbing) or by combining cable car and walk (20 min walk from the arrival station). At the summit, the view embraces the Seto Inland Sea and its dozens of islands. According to tradition, Kōbō Daishi (founder of Shingon Buddhism) lit a flame there 1,200 years ago—it supposedly still burns in one of the summit temples.

visits.text: Yonaha Maehama Beach stretches 7 km of white sand facing emerald-green water—regularly ranked the most beautiful beach in the Orient. Just 15 minutes from Miyako Airport, you can be there right after landing. The seabed is sandy with a gentle slope—perfect for swimming. Arrive early morning to have the beach almost to yourself.

Miyako Marine Park has an underwater observatory 4 meters deep—you descend into a tower and observe tropical fish and sea turtles through portholes, keeping your feet dry. It's a perfect alternative for those who don't want to dive.

Cape Higashi Hennazaki is a narrow 2-km peninsula jutting into the ocean, ranked among Japan's 100 Most Beautiful Parks. The lighthouse at the tip offers 360-degree views of the Pacific on one side and the China Sea on the other. The road there runs along wave-battered cliffs—spectacular in fine weather.

activities.text: Morning flight from Ishigaki to Miyakojima—about 30 minutes. Rent a car at the airport to explore the island. For the return, direct flights connect Miyakojima to Naha (50 minutes) and Tokyo-Haneda (3 hours).

Cross Irabu Bridge (3,540 meters, Japan's longest free bridge) to Irabu Island and continue to Shimoji Beach—surreally blue water, often deserted. The road itself is a spectacle with turquoise water on either side.

Go snorkeling on one of Miyakojima's reefs—the island is ringed by coral and the water clarity is exceptional. Operators offer roughly 1.5-hour outings from Yoshino or Aragusuku Beach. Book the day before in high season.

visits.text: Churaumi Aquarium, opened in 2002 on the site of the 1975 Ocean Expo, is one of the world's largest. Its star is the 7,500-cubic-meter Kuroshio Tank—behind a 22.5-meter-wide by 8.2-meter-tall acrylic panel, an 8.8-meter whale shark has swum here since March 1995, a world record for captivity longevity. The aquarium was also the first in the world to successfully breed manta rays in captivity, in 2007. Whale shark feedings happen at 9:30 AM, 3 PM, and 5 PM.

The Bise Fukugi Tree Road is a shaded one-kilometer path lined with thousands of fukugi trees 10 to 20 meters tall, planted centuries ago as a natural windbreak to protect the village. These dense-leaved trees create a green tunnel leading to a quiet beach facing Ie Island.

The ruins of Nakijin Castle, a UNESCO site, stretch across 1.5 km of walls reaching 8 meters high—the longest of all Okinawan gusuku. This 13th-century castle was the residence of the King of the North during the Sanzan period, before kingdom unification. In January–February, cherry blossoms along the walls make it one of Japan's first hanami spots.

activities.text: Watch the free dolphin show at Okichan Theater in Ocean Expo Park—five performances daily.
Walk or ride a buffalo-drawn cart through the Bise Fukugi Tree Road—the light filtering through the trees creates a peaceful atmosphere. At the path's end, the beach offers views of Ie Island.
Explore Nakijin's 1.5 km of walls on foot—the promenade atop the walls offers views of the East China Sea on one side and forested hills on the other.
