visits.text: Nago Pineapple Park takes you on an automated cart through pineapple fields, tropical greenhouses, and a garden with over 200 varieties of subtropical plants. Okinawan pineapples are smaller but sweeter than what you're used to—try the pineapple wine and ice cream on the way out.

OKINAWA Fruits Land, a few minutes' drive away, is a tropical fruit-themed park with an interactive game trail (the Tropical Kingdom Story) and a café where you can taste seasonal fruit parfaits—mango, dragon fruit, shikuwasa.

The ruins of Nago Castle, on a hill above town, offer panoramic views of Nago Bay and the Motobu Peninsula. In January–February, Kanhizakura cherry blossoms—the first to bloom in Japan—blanket the hill in vivid pink. Even off-season, the climb is worth it for the view.

activities.text: Tour the Orion Brewery in Nago—Orion is THE Okinawan beer, brewed here since 1957. The free guided tour (about 40 minutes) concludes with tasting fresh beers. Book online.
Ride the automated cart through Pineapple Park's plantation—the driverless vehicle cruises through rows of outdoor pineapple plants.
Stroll Nago's streets in the evening—the town is much quieter than Naha and retains an authentic local atmosphere. The small soba restaurants and izakaya are worth seeking out.

days_plan.itin_title: Okinawa and Ryukyu Islands Journey: Beaches and Culture

visits.text: Kokusai-dori, Naha's main artery, stretches 1.6 km between the bus terminal and Makishi Station. Dubbed the "Miracle Mile" because it rose from wartime ashes within just a few years, it owes its name to the Ernie Pyle International Theater, a cinema built for American soldiers after 1945. Around 600 shops line the street—souvenir stores, soba restaurants, awamori bars. On Sundays from noon to 6 PM, the street closes to traffic and street performers take over. Go in the evening when the atmosphere really comes alive.

Makishi Public Market, nicknamed the "Kitchen of Okinawa," has existed since 1950—born as a black market during the American occupation. After four years of renovation, it reopened in March 2023 in a brand-new three-story building. On the ground floor, you'll find tropical fish in improbable colors, Agu pork, and sea grapes. Buy your ingredients downstairs and have them cooked on the second floor—this is the famous "mochiage" system. If the vendor throws in a little something extra when you pay, that's the "shibun," a gesture of generosity typical of Okinawa.

Step into the Tsuboya district through the Heiwa-dori arcade: a 400-meter street paved with Ryukyu limestone awaits, lined with some twenty pottery workshops. The district was born in 1682, when the royal government consolidated all the scattered pottery kilns across the island into one location. For over 340 years, artisans have been producing yachimun—glazed pottery fired at 1,200°C. Don't miss the Tsuboya Pottery Museum at the entrance, which houses pieces dating back 6,700 years.

Naminoue Shrine perches on a coral cliff above Naha's only beach. Its name means "above the waves." Before becoming a Shinto shrine, the site was an utaki dedicated to Nirai Kanai, the mythical paradise beyond the sea. The king himself came here to pray every New Year. Destroyed in 1945, the main hall was rebuilt in 1953. A highway bridge somewhat spoils the view from the beach, but walk onto that bridge for the best vantage point of the vermillion shrine perched on the cliff.

The covered shopping arcades of Heiwa-dori, Mutsumi-dori, and Ichiba Hondori form a labyrinth of alleys perpendicular to Kokusai-dori. You'll find shisa statues, colorful kariyushi shirts, artisanal awamori, and beni-imo tarts. Prices are often negotiable—a smile might earn you a little gift on top.

Shuri Castle served as the palace of the Ryukyu Kingdom for 450 years, from 1429 to 1879. A unique blend of Chinese and Japanese architecture, it has been destroyed five times—in 1453, 1660, 1709, during the Battle of Okinawa in 1945, and by fire in October 2019 that consumed seven buildings in 11 hours. Reconstruction of the main hall (Seiden) is underway and should be completed by autumn 2026—you can watch the construction from an observation deck and see artisans laying the 60,000 roof tiles.

Shureimon Gate, printed on the 2,000-yen bill, stands at the castle entrance. Built in the 16th century by King Shō Sei, it bears the inscription "Land of Propriety" in classical Chinese. Destroyed during the war, rebuilt in 1958, it survived the 2019 fire.

Tamaudun Royal Mausoleum, a five-minute walk away, is the tomb of the second Shō dynasty built in 1501 by King Shō Shin. It houses the remains of 18 kings in a massive stone structure divided into three chambers. Designated a National Treasure in 2018 and a UNESCO site since 2000.

The Kinjo-chō stone pavement descends 300 meters through a quiet residential neighborhood lined with large akagi trees over 200 years old. This was the main road to the castle in the 16th century. The atmosphere is completely different—here, you're in residential Shuri.

Kouri Bridge soars 1,960 meters over turquoise waters to tiny Kouri Island (8 km circumference). The water below is so intensely blue that locals call it "Kouri Blue." On the island, seek out Heart Rock—two heart-shaped rocks on Tinu Beach, now a famous romantic spot.

Cape Hedo marks the northernmost point of Okinawa's main island. Steep coral cliffs plunge into rough ocean where the Pacific meets the East China Sea. On clear days, you'll glimpse Yoron and Izena Islands offshore. The site is windswept and wild—a striking contrast with the southern beaches.

Yanbaru Forest, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2021, covers northern Okinawa's densely wooded hills. It's the territory of the Okinawa rail (yanbaru kuina), a flightless bird threatened with extinction. Daisekirinzan Park offers 30- to 60-minute trails through impressive karst formations.

activities.text: Taste awamori, Okinawa's traditional alcohol distilled from rice with black kōji yeast—it ranges from 25 to 60% ABV. The bravest will try habushu, awamori infused with a habu viper. Several restaurants on Kokusai-dori offer tastings.

Catch Saturday evening street performances on Kokusai-dori: traditional dancers, sanshin musicians, fire breathers. Most shops stay open until 11 PM—the atmosphere is far livelier in the evening than during the day.

Join a yachimun pottery workshop in Tsuboya—allow 60 to 90 minutes to mold your own shisa, Okinawa's guardian lion-dog. Shisa come in pairs: mouth open to chase away evil spirits, mouth closed to keep in the good. Your creation will be mailed to you after firing. Book online in advance.
Visit the underground museum at Tamaudun (included in the ticket) to understand the Ryukyuan senkotsu funeral ritual—bones were washed and placed in urns several years after death.
Watch artisans at work on the Seiden reconstruction site from the observation platform. The castle's bright red lacquering requires dozens of successive coats.

Discover bingata dyeing at Shuri Ryusen workshop, just minutes from the castle. This 500-year-old textile art uses vivid colors inspired by tropical nature—you can dye your own handkerchief. Allow about an hour; reservation recommended.
Drive across Kouri Bridge—the view of the turquoise water below is breathtaking. On the other side, explore the island's sugarcane fields and small deserted beaches.

After visiting the north, drive back to Naha (about 2 hours via Okinawa Expressway) to spend the night before tomorrow's flight to Ishigaki. If departing from Cape Hedo, allow at least 2.5 hours to the airport.

visits.text: Lake Okushima (Shima Dam), 4km in circumference, is famous for its intense turquoise color nicknamed "Shima Blue." This tint comes from mineral particles dissolved in water from surrounding volcanic springs. On sunny days, the water is so clear you can see the bottom—and the blue shifts toward emerald depending on the light. Best season to see it: spring (April-May), when the still-tender vegetation contrasts with the turquoise. In autumn, red maples around the lake add spectacular contrast.

Shima Onsen, the neighboring hot spring resort, has a history spanning over 500 years. Its waters are reputed to cure "40,000 ailments" (shi-man = 40,000 in Japanese, hence the name). The old thermal quarter, with its stone alleys and wooden ryokan, has a retro Shōwa charm—some say Miyazaki's Spirited Away drew partial inspiration from it.

activities.text: Treat yourself to a night in a Shima Onsen ryokan. The outdoor baths (rotenburo) with river views are blissful in every season—in winter, steam rises into the frozen air and it's almost surreal. Several ryokan offer baths open to day visitors if you're not staying overnight.

days_plan.itin_title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey

visits.text: Sefa Utaki is the most sacred site of the Ryukyu Kingdom, a UNESCO site since 2000. There are no buildings here—it's the rocks and forest that are venerated. When you reach the Sangui, a triangular tunnel formed by two massive rocks, you'll understand why priestesses came here to glimpse the sacred island of Kudaka. This is where the kingdom's highest priestess was enthroned. Buy tickets at the Nanjo local products center, 500 meters from the entrance.

The Valley of Gangala formed when limestone caves collapsed, creating a 48,000-square-meter chasm covered in subtropical forest. The Ufushu Gajumaru, a giant banyan tree 20 meters tall estimated at 150 years old, is the highlight. The world's oldest fishhook was found here—23,000 years old. The 80-minute guided tour is the only way to enter—book online the day before, with four daily departures (10 AM, noon, 2 PM, 4 PM).

Okinawa World houses Gyokusendo Cave—Okinawa's largest, 5 km long with 890 meters open to the public. Formed 300,000 years ago, it contains over a million stalactites that grow 1 mm every three years thanks to the subtropical climate. Constant temperature of 21°C—a refuge on hot days.

activities.text: Have coffee in Sakitari Cave at the entrance to the Valley of Gangala—the bar is set up under stalactites in what was once a prehistoric dwelling. Beverage included in the tour price.
At Okinawa World's Ryukyu Kingdom Village, watch an Eisa performance—traditional Okinawan drum and dance. Multiple shows daily.
Make a detour to the Nirai Kanai Bridge overlook—the view plunges down to the coast and Pacific, one of southern Okinawa's finest panoramas.

days_plan.itin_title: Okinawa and Ryukyu Islands Journey: Beaches and Culture

visits.text: The Naruko Gorge extends for 4 km along the Otani River west of the spa village. The cliffs rise to 100 meters high and the valley width varies from 10 to 100 meters, creating a characteristic V-shaped canyon. The best viewpoint is the observation platform at Narukokyo Resthouse, from which you can spot the Ofukazawa Bridge crossing the canyon at mid-height. It's this bridge, photographed with the flaming foliage from mid-October to early November, that has become the gorge's symbol. Arrive early in autumn - tour buses start arriving from 9am.

Naruko Onsen is one of Tohoku's oldest hot spring resorts, appreciated since the 9th century according to chronicles. The spa village forms with four neighboring resorts the 'Naruko Onsenkyo,' which together has nearly 400 springs of ten different water types - eight of the ten types recognized in Japan are present here, which is exceptional. Naruko's distinctive feature is that many inns and communal baths have their own individual spring.

activities.text: Naruko is the birthplace of Naruko-style kokeshi dolls, one of Japan's three great kokeshi traditions. These turned wooden dolls, with chrysanthemum motifs, have a 200-year history and are designated a national traditional craft. Their special feature: the head squeaks when you turn it, thanks to a unique assembly technique. At the Japan Kokeshi Museum, on the road to the gorge, you can watch artisans at work and paint your own kokeshi.

Put on a yukata at your ryokan and go 'onsen hopping' in the village. The foot baths (ashiyu) on the main street are free and open to all - a good pretext for striking up conversation with locals. Kokeshi-dôri (Kokeshi Street) lines up artisan shops and galleries where you can watch dolls being crafted live.

At Onikobe, 30 minutes by bus from Naruko Onsen, the Benten geyser erupts a 15-meter column of water every 10 minutes - one of Japan's rare natural geysers. Nearby Jigokudani ('hell valley') offers landscapes of steam and boiling rock worthy of the name. The Oku no Hosomichi trail (9 km) that poet Matsuo Bashô followed in 1689 crosses the entire region - a combined cultural and natural hike.

days_plan.itin_title: Tohoku Lakes & Volcanoes Circuit

visits.text: Kegon Falls (97 meters) is one of Japan's three great waterfalls. Water from Lake Chūzenji plunges into a chasm surrounded by rock walls—the roar reaches you well before you arrive. A paid elevator carved through rock descends you to the observation platform at the falls' base—from there, you feel the spray and the water's raw power. In autumn (October), maples frame the cascade in crimson and gold. In winter, the falls freeze partially—the spectacle of blue ice is rare and striking.

Lake Chūzenji, at 1,269 meters altitude, formed about 20,000 years ago following an eruption of Mount Nantai. It's Japan's largest high-altitude lake. Take a boat tour to admire the forested shores—in autumn, colors reflect on the water like a mirror.

Descend toward central Nikko to visit Tōshō-gū, the mausoleum of Tokugawa Ieyasu (first shogun of the Edo period), completed in 1636 and UNESCO-listed. It's Japan's most ornate shrine—15,000 workers labored on it. Don't miss the Yōmeimon gate, covered with over 500 painted and gilded carvings, and the famous three monkeys ("see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil") carved on the sacred stable.

activities.text: Upon arriving in Nikko, stop at Shinkyō bridge (the sacred bridge), lacquered vermilion, spanning the Daiya River at the site entrance. According to legend, the monk Shōdō Shōnin crossed the river in 766 on the backs of two serpents—the bridge marks this spot. The classic photo is taken from the roadside below, with the cedar forest as backdrop.

days_plan.itin_title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey

visits.text: The Nunobiki Plateau, perched at 1,080 meters altitude south of Lake Inawashiro, hosts one of Japan's largest wind farms: 33 Enercon E-70 turbines standing 100 meters tall generate 66 megawatts - enough to power approximately 35,000 households. The facility entered service in February 2007. The panoramic view from the plateau embraces Lake Inawashiro, Mount Bandai, and the Azuma peaks. Bring warm clothes even in summer: the wind is constant and the temperature cool.

From early August to early September, the plateau becomes covered with approximately 200,000 sunflowers planted in three successive waves - visit between August 10th and 25th for peak bloom. The yellow flowers facing the 100-meter windmills create one of Fukushima Prefecture's most photographed tableaux. Don't leave without tasting the Nunobiki Plateau daikon radish sold at local stands - a specialty renowned throughout the region.

activities.text: Marked trails allow you to walk among the windmills and flower fields. In autumn, the sunflowers give way to purple and pink cosmos. The observatory at the far end of the plateau offers a 360° view that hikers of all levels can reach without particular effort.

Lake Inawashiro - Japan's fourth largest lake - stands out for its intensely blue waters and reflection of Mount Bandai. If you descend from the plateau toward the lake, stop at the Hideyo Noguchi Museum, dedicated to the Japanese doctor born in Inawashiro in 1876 who became a national figure in bacteriology: his portrait appears on the thousand-yen bill.

days_plan.itin_title: Tohoku Lakes & Volcanoes Circuit

visits.text: Stroll through Korakuen Garden, celebrated as one of Japan's three most beautiful landscape gardens, featuring meticulously designed lawns, ponds, and groves created over 300 years ago. The garden uniquely incorporates sweeping lawns rare in traditional Japanese design, along with plum and cherry groves, tea fields, and crane aviaries. Views across the pond to black Okayama Castle complete the scenery.

Explore Okayama Castle, nicknamed 'Crow Castle' for its striking black exterior, contrasting dramatically with the white castles typical of Japan. Originally built in 1597, the reconstructed castle houses a museum of local history and offers panoramic views from its top floor. The castle's location on the Asahi River creates beautiful reflections and photo opportunities.

Wander through the historic Bikan Quarter of Kurashiki, where white-walled warehouses with distinctive black tiles line a willow-draped canal, creating one of Japan's most picturesque townscapes. These Edo-period storehouses, once used for rice and cotton trade, now house museums, boutiques, and cafés. Punt boat rides along the canal offer romantic views of this perfectly preserved merchant district.

activities.text: Visit the Ohara Museum of Art in Kurashiki, Japan's first private museum of Western art, housing an impressive collection including works by El Greco, Monet, and Picasso. The Greek-inspired building stands as an unexpected cultural treasure in this traditional merchant town. The museum complex also features Asian antiquities and contemporary Japanese art galleries.

Discover the ancient Kibitsu Shrine with its unique architecture and famous 400-meter covered corridor connecting to the Kibitsu-jinja shrine. This atmospheric structure, designated a National Treasure, inspired the folk tale of Momotaro (Peach Boy), one of Japan's most beloved legends. The shrine is especially beautiful during the hydrangea season.

Explore the charming shops and galleries of Kurashiki's Bikan district, where traditional crafts, denim products (the region is Japan's denim capital), and local delicacies await discovery. The preserved buildings create a unique shopping experience unlike anywhere else in Japan. Evening illuminations transform the canal district into a romantic wonderland.

San'in Secret Coast, Japan: Dunes, Shrines and Coastal Memory

visits.text: Cape Manzamo is a 20-meter coral cliff whose profile evokes an elephant's trunk—it's Okinawa's most famous postcard image. Its name means "grassland where ten thousand people can sit," bestowed by King Shō Kei in the 18th century. Come early morning or late afternoon to avoid tour buses.
The Blue Cave (Aoi Dōkutsu) at Maeda Point is a sea cavern where sunlight penetrates from below and tints the water electric blue. It's Okinawa's most popular diving and snorkeling site.

Ryukyu Mura is a reconstructed village with traditional Okinawan houses—some over 100 years old—dismantled and reassembled in a subtropical garden. You'll see eisa dance performances, sanshin folk songs, and demonstrations of old-fashioned sugarcane processing.

activities.text: Go snorkeling or diving in the Blue Cave—operators offer roughly 2-hour outings, transport and equipment included. Book the day before, especially in summer. Conditions are best in the morning when light enters the cave.
Paint your own ceramic shisa at Ryukyu Mura and try on traditional Ryukyu costume for a souvenir photo. Kids love the water buffalo trail.
Catch the folk song and sanshin performance at Ryukyu Mura—musicians play in the traditional houses, creating an intimate atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Okinawa and Ryukyu Islands Journey: Beaches and Culture

visits.text: Explore magnificent Osaka Castle, one of Japan's most famous landmarks rising majestically from a vast park surrounded by impressive stone walls and moats. The reconstructed castle houses an extensive museum chronicling the life of Toyotomi Hideyoshi and the castle's dramatic history. The observation deck offers panoramic views across Osaka, and the grounds are spectacular during cherry blossom season.

Immerse yourself in the electric atmosphere of Dotonbori, Osaka's legendary entertainment district where giant neon signs, including the iconic Glico Running Man, reflect on the Dotonbori Canal. This sensory overload of lights, sounds, and incredible food represents Osaka's reputation as Japan's kitchen. The area comes alive at night with street performers and endless dining options.

Browse the bustling stalls of Kuromon Ichiba Market, Osaka's 'Kitchen' for over 190 years, where chefs and food lovers seek the freshest seafood, produce, and prepared delicacies. Sample giant grilled scallops, sea urchin, Kobe beef skewers, and the region's best sashimi as you wander the covered arcade. The market offers one of Japan's most authentic and delicious food experiences.

Experience Expo 2025 Osaka, a world's fair bringing together innovations, cultures, and visions for the future from nations around the globe. The exposition showcases cutting-edge technology, sustainable solutions, and creative pavilions designed by world-renowned architects. This once-in-a-lifetime event transforms Yumeshima Island into a celebration of human achievement and international cooperation.

Wander through Kobe's charming Kitano-cho district, where over 20 elegantly preserved ijinkan (foreign residences) recall the city's cosmopolitan history as an international trading port. These Victorian and colonial-era mansions, built by Western merchants in the late 19th century, now serve as museums showcasing European furniture, art, and lifestyles. The hillside neighborhood offers beautiful views and a distinctly European atmosphere unique in Japan.

Soar above the city on the Kobe Nunobiki Ropeway to the Herb Gardens, a fragrant paradise of themed gardens offering panoramic views of Kobe, the harbor, and Osaka Bay. The expansive gardens feature over 200 varieties of herbs and flowers, a German-style restaurant, and seasonal events. The views from the observation plaza are especially magical at sunset.

Explore Kobe Harborland, a stylish waterfront development featuring the iconic Kobe Port Tower, historic red-brick warehouses, and the giant Ferris wheel offering bay views. This entertainment district combines shopping, dining, and maritime attractions in a scenic harbor setting. Evening illuminations create a romantic atmosphere along the waterfront promenade.

activities.text: Ascend the futuristic Umeda Sky Building, an architectural marvel with its distinctive floating garden observatory connecting twin towers 40 stories above ground. The 360-degree open-air deck offers spectacular views of Osaka's urban sprawl, particularly stunning at sunset when the city lights begin twinkling. The basement food court recreates a nostalgic early 20th-century streetscape.

Discover the atmospheric Shinsekai district, a retro entertainment quarter preserving the atmosphere of early 20th-century Osaka, crowned by the iconic Tsutenkaku Tower. This nostalgic neighborhood is famous for kushikatsu (deep-fried skewers) served in boisterous old-school restaurants. The area offers an authentic taste of working-class Osaka culture rarely experienced by tourists.

Visit the vibrant Namba Yasaka Shrine, instantly recognizable for its giant lion head stage where the enormous mouth serves as a performance space. This unique architectural feature is believed to swallow evil spirits and bring good fortune, making it popular with those seeking luck in business and exams. The shrine offers one of Osaka's most unusual and photogenic sights.

Explore the diverse national pavilions at Expo 2025, each offering unique architectural expressions and interactive exhibits representing their country's innovations and cultural heritage. From virtual reality experiences to sustainable technology demonstrations, the pavilions provide fascinating glimpses into global progress. Allow a full day to appreciate the scope and variety of exhibits.

Sample international cuisines at the Expo's food zones, featuring restaurants and food stalls representing culinary traditions from participating nations. The fair offers rare opportunities to taste authentic dishes from countries rarely represented in Japan. Food and cultural performances combine to create a truly global experience.

Attend performances and events at the Expo's entertainment venues, featuring artists, musicians, and cultural presentations from around the world. Evening illuminations and special shows transform the Expo site into a spectacular display of light and creativity. The combination of technology, art, and international exchange creates unforgettable experiences.

Savor world-famous Kobe beef at one of the city's renowned teppanyaki restaurants, where master chefs prepare exquisitely marbled wagyu before your eyes. The legendary beef, raised from Tajima cattle under strict standards, offers unparalleled tenderness and flavor. Lunch courses offer more affordable ways to experience this culinary treasure.

Visit the ancient Ikuta Shrine, one of Japan's oldest Shinto shrines dating back 1,800 years, tucked amid Kobe's modern downtown. The shrine's peaceful forest survived the 1995 earthquake and provides a spiritual oasis in the bustling city center. The shrine is popular for weddings and offers unique charms and fortunes.

Stroll through Nankinmachi, Kobe's vibrant Chinatown, one of only three major Chinatowns in Japan, featuring ornate gates, temples, and countless restaurants. The compact district buzzes with food stalls selling steamed buns, dumplings, and Chinese sweets. The area reflects Kobe's long history as an international trading port.

visits.text: Otaru Canal — Historic 1.3-kilometer canal built between 1914 and 1923 for maritime transport, lined with red brick warehouses converted into shops and restaurants, 63 vintage gas lamps lit at dusk creating romantic atmosphere, iconic Asakusa bridge, winter stroll with snow sculptures, symbol of past port prosperity.

Sakaimachi Street — Historic 900-meter shopping street preserving Meiji and Taisho architecture, 70 shops in renovated century-old buildings, specialty Otaru Orgel-do music boxes with 25,000 models on display, mouth-blown artisanal glassware, LeTao pastries famous for double cheese, nostalgic 1920s atmosphere.

Otaru Music Box Museum — Largest music box museum in Japan housed in 1912 warehouse, collection of 3,400 antique and contemporary music boxes from around the world, customization workshop to create your own box, regular concerts of historic 1908 steam organ, extensive shop, outdoor steam clock chiming every 15 minutes.

Temiya Shrine — Shinto shrine perched on hill offering panoramic view of port and Ishikari Bay, founded in 1865 to protect sailors and fishermen, steep staircase of 105 steps under vermillion torii, simple traditional wooden architecture, peaceful atmosphere away from tourist areas, cherry blossoms in May, popular spot for New Year's first visit hatsumode.

activities.text: Visit to an artisanal glassworks — Attend a glass blowing demonstration in one of Otaru's 18 workshops perpetuating a century-old tradition, glass heated to 1200 degrees shaped by master craftsmen, possibility to create your own object in 30 minutes under supervision, technique inherited from European importation during the Meiji era, shops selling unique colored lamps bowls and cups.

Tasting at Sankaku Market — Explore the triangular fish market open since 1950, stalls of king crabs and fresh sea urchins caught in the morning, restaurants offering kaisendon hearty breakfast from 6am, lively local atmosphere, reasonable prices compared to Tokyo, free sample tasting, shops selling dried products and preserves.

days_plan.itin_title: Hokkaido's Summer Road Trip: Furano Lavender, Shikotsu-Tōya Parks & Coast

visits.text: The Oze Marshlands, listed under the Ramsar Convention, form a high-altitude wetland zone 2km by 6km tucked between Mount Shibutsu and Mount Hiuchigatake at about 1,400 meters elevation. It's one of Japan's last great pristine marshes. Wooden boardwalks (mokudō) traverse the marsh—you literally walk above the water and peat. In summer (June-July), Nikko daylilies (Nikko-kisuge) carpet the meadows in yellow, with snow-capped Mount Shibutsu as backdrop. In spring, white cotton grass (mizubasho) transforms the marsh into a cotton field.

activities.text: Accommodation is in mountain huts (sansō)—book ahead in high season. The huts are simple but clean, with meals included. Bring good hiking boots and water—there are no vending machines on the trails. The return can be made via a different trail to vary the scenery.

days_plan.itin_title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey

visits.text: Rebun Island measures 29 km north to south and 8 km wide - it's Japan's northernmost island excluding uninhabited islets. Its nickname is the "floating island of flowers": about 300 alpine plant varieties grow here at sea level, a phenomenon unique in the world due to the island's particular arctic climate. Among them, the Rebun-atsumoriso - a pale lady's slipper growing nowhere else on Earth, flowering from late May to mid-June. Cape Sukoton, at the northern tip, offers views of the uninhabited Todo islet where sea lions laze, and in clear weather to Sakhalin.

Momoiwa Observatory, in the island's south, is accessible via the 7 km trail running along the west coast from Kafuka port. This 3h30 route crosses cliff meadows covered with alpine flowers with Mount Rishiri's silhouette permanently visible behind you over the sea. Endemic Rebun edelweiss (Rebun-usuyukiso, Leontopodium discolor), the island's official flower, carpets the slopes. Momoiwa rock ("peach rock," 250 m) and Neko rock ("cat rock") animate this coastal landscape.

activities.text: The Momoiwa trail is Rebun's emblematic hike: 7 km west coast, 3h30 walk, accessible to all levels. It departs from Kafuka ferry terminal, crosses the Momoiwa promontory, runs along Motochi cliffs, passes before Jizo rock (50 m, resembling a bodhisattva in prayer) and reaches Shiretoko village in the south. Bus returns to port. Prefer a windless day - cliffs are exposed.

For ambitious hikers, the 8-hour route connects Cape Sukoton (north) to Shiretoko (south) over 20 km, crossing the entire island via ridges. Simultaneous views of both coasts at pass summits. Early morning departure mandatory, bring provisions.

Kita no Canaria Park, in the island's north, preserves the wooden school filmed for the Japanese feature film "Northern Canaries" (2012). The view from the café veranda of Mount Rishiri rising from the sea is one of the island's most romantic. Rebun sea urchins and hoke (local mackerel) as sashimi are specialties to taste at Kafuka port restaurants.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: Mount Rishiri (1,721 m) is a solitary stratovolcano rising from the Sea of Japan, ranked among Japan's 100 famous mountains and nicknamed "Rishiri-Fuji" for its perfect conical shape. From the ferry, the snow-capped cone emerging from blue waters recalls Mount Fuji - but surrounded by water on all sides. Its name comes from Ainu and means "high island." In summer, endemic alpine flowers grow on its flanks, including the Rishiri poppy (Rishiri-hinageshi) that grows nowhere else in the world. The complete ascent takes 10 to 12 hours round trip - reserved for experienced hikers and in good weather only.

Hime-numa Pond, in the island's north near Oshidomari, is an ornithological observation site nestled at the volcano's foot. In calm weather, Mount Rishiri's perfect silhouette reflects in its waters - the island's most photographed image. Otatomari Pond, in the south, offers the same composition in a wilder setting, surrounded by Sakhalin spruce forest.

Cape Peshi, next to Oshidomari port, is reached in 15 minutes walking from the ferry terminal. From this 93-meter promontory, the view of the port, the Sea of Japan, and Mount Rishiri's cone in the background is striking. It's also one of the island's best spots for sunset.

activities.text: Leave your car in Wakkanai - bringing a vehicle on the ferry costs 22,000 to 30,000 yen one way. Rather rent a bike or car on the island upon arrival at Oshidomari. The dedicated bike path runs along the north coast for 20 km with spectacular views of the Sea of Japan. For the complete island circuit (60 km), plan a full day by bike.

If you're fit, Mount Rishiri ascent is the island's experience. Departure before 5am from the Oshidomari trailhead, allow 10 to 12 hours round trip. From the summit, 360° views of the island, Rebun, Hokkaido, and in clear weather to Sakhalin in Russia. The climbing season runs from late June to early October. Filing a climbing plan is recommended.

Don't leave Rishiri without tasting sea urchins (uni) and konbu (kelp seaweed) - both reputed among Japan's finest, nourished in the cold rich waters around the island. Oshidomari port restaurants offer rice bowls with fresh morning urchins. A sea urchin harvesting experience is organized at Cape Kamui for visitors.

visits.text: Odori Park is Sapporo's heart: a 1.5 km boulevard-park divided into 13 sections running east to west, built in 1871 as the city's firebreak. At its eastern end, the TV Tower (147 m) offers a bird's-eye view of the city's orthogonal grid - modeled on North American cities during Hokkaido's colonization in the Meiji era. The park hosts all the city's major events: Snow Festival in February, beer garden in summer, autumn food festival in September.

The Former Hokkaido Government Office - the Akarenga ("red brick") - is an American neo-Baroque edifice from 1888, emblematic of the island's colonization period. Its red bricks, green dome, and gardens make it one of Sapporo's most photographed buildings. The interior houses an exhibition on Hokkaido's history and admission is free.

The Clock Tower (Tokeidai), built in 1878, is one of Sapporo's three oldest historic buildings. Oddly squeezed between modern buildings downtown, it sometimes disappoints visitors expecting more grandeur - and that's precisely what makes it amusing to see. The interior is a small museum on the city's history.

The Sapporo Beer Museum occupies the old red-brick brewery from 1890 - Japan's first modern brewery, founded by Seibei Nakagawa upon his return from studies in Germany. The building crowned with a five-pointed red polar star has become a city symbol. The tour explains how Sapporo became one of Japan's three most consumed beers. Tasting available on-site at tour's end.

Mount Moiwa (531 m) is reached by ropeway from downtown. Its observatory offers the most complete night view of Sapporo: the city's illuminated grid stretches to snow-capped mountains on the horizon. Ranked among Japan's 100 best night views. Ascend 30 minutes before sunset to witness the transition from day to night.

Hokkaido Shrine in Maruyama Park is Hokkaido's principal Shinto shrine, founded in 1869 to watch over the island's colonization. The Maruyama primeval forest surrounding it - one of the rare intact ancient forests within a major Japanese city - shelters squirrels and birds. In spring, 1,400 cherry trees bloom around the shrine.

activities.text: Start the day at Nijo Market from 7am. It's Sapporo's kitchen: hairy crabs (kegani) and king crabs, sea urchins, scallops, and Hokkaido salmon line the stalls. Order a sanshoku-don - a rice bowl topped with sea urchin, salmon roe, and crab - at one of the restaurants bordering the market. It's the city's best value.

In the evening, dive into Susukino - the largest entertainment district north of Tokyo. Izakaya, beer bars, late-night ramen: the atmosphere electrifies from 8pm. Don't miss Ramen Yokocho, the ramen alley with 17 restaurants packed together - this is where Sapporo miso ramen was popularized in the 1950s. Choose randomly: all addresses are good.

Climb to Hitsujigaoka Observation Hill ("sheep hill") where a statue of Dr. William Clark, founder of Hokkaido Agricultural University, points toward the horizon with his famous phrase: "Boys, be ambitious!" The view of Sapporo and the Daisetsuzan mountains in clear weather ranks among the city's best. Sheep actually graze on the site.

Genghis Khan (jingisukan) is THE Sapporo specialty you won't easily find elsewhere in Japan: lamb grilled on a Mongolian helmet-shaped grill, accompanied by sautéed vegetables and soy sauce. It's the typical meal for residents' celebrations and summer barbecues. Look for a restaurant with a queue - that's usually a good sign.

visits.text: Lake Saroma is Hokkaido's largest lake and Japan's third (154 km²), separated from the Sea of Okhotsk by a sand spit. It's a brackish lake - a mixture of fresh water and seawater entering through two openings in the spit. From Mount Horoiwa observatory (376 m), accessible in 1 hour hiking, the view of the lake's blue expanse and coastal spit provides scale of what is actually a gigantic marine lagoon. The sunset from this lookout - sky and lake merging in orange and purple hues - is one of Hokkaido's most beautiful.

Cape Kimuanepp juts into Lake Saroma from the north shore, at the coastal spit's end. In autumn (mid-September to early October), a glasswort variety (akkeshiso) reddens and covers the ground with a crimson carpet - a rare phenomenon also visible at Notsuke and around Lake Toro. The contrast between the coral grass's flaming red and the lake's blue background is photogenic.

activities.text: Hike or bike in Wakka Garden (Wakka Gensei-kaen), at the sand spit's eastern end, one of Japan's richest dune floras with over 300 plant species. The Nature Center lends bikes for free (season April to mid-October). The trail runs along the Okhotsk on one side and Lake Saroma on the other - views of both water bodies simultaneously.

Lake Saroma is renowned for its farmed scallops and oysters, whose quality is recognized throughout Japan. Lakeside restaurants serve shell-on scallops, grilled or raw as sashimi, with a flavor made incomparable by proximity to the sea. A specialty not to miss if passing through.

In winter, Lake Saroma partially freezes and hosts ice smelt fishing. Whooper swans migrate from Siberia and rest on non-frozen zones - up to several hundred birds simultaneously. The sunset over the lake with swan silhouettes is an unparalleled winter spectacle.

visits.text: Cape Kamui juts into the Sea of Japan on the Shakotan Peninsula, within Niseko-Shakotan-Otaru Kaigan Quasi-National Park. Ainu legend tells that Charenka, daughter of an Ainu chief in love with the warrior Minamoto no Yoshitsune, threw herself into the sea when he abandoned her - her spirit supposedly petrified into the Kamui rock, 40 meters high, visible at the cape's tip. Following this, women were historically forbidden access until the Meiji era. You can still see the "Gate Forbidden to Women" at the Charenka trail's beginning.

The Shimamui coast, a few kilometers north of Cape Kamui, offers maximum concentration of the famous "Shakotan Blue" - this deep turquoise hue resulting from exceptionally clear waters. Less crowded than the cape itself, it deserves a stop for photographers. The view from the lookout overlooking the cliffs ranks among the peninsula's finest.

activities.text: Hike the 770-meter Charenka trail to the cape's tip: allow 20 minutes one way, 40 minutes round trip. The trail is developed but rugged in places. At the point, enjoy 300-degree panoramic views of the Sea of Japan's curved horizon. Bring a windbreaker - gusts are powerful. The cape is accessible from spring to autumn only, the gate being closed off-season.

In early summer (June-July), Ezo daylilies (Ezo Kanzou) bloom in orange-yellow clusters along the trail, contrasting with the sea blue below. This ranks among Hokkaido's most photographed panoramas. Shakotan Blue is particularly intense in sunny weather between 10am and 2pm, when the sun is high.

The Shakotan Peninsula is renowned for its sea urchins (uni), considered among Japan's finest. Seaside restaurants serve rice bowls covered with fresh urchins caught that morning. The season runs from May to August - don't miss this opportunity if you're there in summer.

visits.text: The Shiretoko Peninsula - "sir-etok," the end of the world in Ainu - has been listed as a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2005. No road penetrates the final 50 kilometers of the peninsula, which concentrate 80% of Hokkaido's brown bear population. The Shiretoko Five Lakes (Shiretoko Goko) are five small volcanic lakes formed hundreds of years ago by an eruption of Mount Io, connected by primeval Ezo spruce forest. A free elevated 800-meter boardwalk provides access to Lake Ichiko with views of the Sea of Okhotsk. Ground trails of 1.6 km to 3 km allow completing the five-lake loop (90 minutes) - but guide mandatory mid-May to late July due to high bear activity.

Furepe Falls plunges directly from a cliff into the Sea of Okhotsk from a 2 km coastal trail from the nature center. The Shiretoko mountain range reflects in the calm lakes at the waterfall's foot - one of the peninsula's most refined compositions. Brown bears sometimes descend to the pebble beaches below.

Shiretoko Pass (Shiretoko Toge) at 740 meters connects Utoro (west coast) to Rausu (east coast). The view of Mount Rausu (1,661 m), the peninsula's highest point, and in clear weather the Russian island of Kunashiri across the Nemuro Strait, is striking. It's also one of Japan's first places to see autumn colors - early October.

activities.text: Book a boat cruise from Utoro Port (April to November) that runs along the peninsula's wild coast. Brown bears on beaches, seals on rocks, white-tailed sea eagles in the air - the wildlife concentration is exceptional. The 3-hour cruises to Kashuni no Taki (waterfall falling into the sea) are most complete.

Five Lakes hike: if you're there between mid-May and late July, book a certified guide (about 5,000 yen, groups of 10 maximum, 3h duration). Outside bear season, the complete 3 km loop is freely accessible with a brief safety orientation. Bring a bear bell, carry no food on the trail.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: The Sounkyo Gorge - whose name comes from Ainu and means "river with many waterfalls" - stretches 24 kilometers along the Ishikari River. The basaltic cliffs reach 100 meters in height, formed by magma that cooled millions of years ago. The Obako and Kobako formations, where the walls narrow into a tight corridor, are most spectacular. Sounkyo is one of Japan's first sites to display autumn colors - from mid-September at Mount Kurodake's summit, then progressively toward the valley floor until mid-October.

Ginga no Taki ("Milky Way") and Ryusei no Taki ("Shooting Star") Falls are 3 km east of the village. They drop directly from 100-meter cliffs in two opposing styles: Ginga is thin, silvery, and spread like hair - the "female fall" - while Ryusei is powerful, compact, and direct - the "male fall." A viewpoint allows seeing both simultaneously.

activities.text: Rent an electric bike in the village to ride up the gorge to the falls and Obako-Kobako formations. The road follows the Ishikari River for about 8 km one way. Rental shops are near downtown Sounkyo. In peak color season (mid-September to mid-October), the contrast between dark basaltic cliffs and red-and-gold foliage is striking.

Take the Kurodake ropeway from Sounkyo visitor center to access the national park heights. A chairlift extends the journey to within an hour's walk from Mount Kurodake summit (1,984 m). From the top, view the steam plumes of Asahidake on the horizon. A natural onsen is accessible during descent.

The public onsen Kurodake no Yu in downtown is Sounkyo's communal hot spring bath - separate indoor and outdoor baths, modest rate, mountain view from the outdoor bath. In winter (late January to late March), the town hosts the Ice Waterfall Festival (Hyobaku Matsuri) with giant snow and ice sculptures.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: Showa Kinen Park (Shōwa Kinen Kōen) is a vast 180-hectare space created to commemorate Emperor Shōwa's reign. Its autumn centerpiece: a 200-meter ginkgo avenue whose golden leaves form a golden tunnel in November. In spring, cherry blossoms and tulips take over; in summer, sunflowers; in autumn, cosmos. If you have children, the immense playground and picnic areas make it an excellent family park.

For underground enthusiasts, Nippara Limestone Cave (Nippara Shōnyūdō) in Okutama is one of Japan's seven great caves—800 meters of galleries with formations illuminated by colored LEDs. The "Shidenorama" formation (calcite curtain) is particularly impressive. Interior temperature is constant around 11°C—bring a layer.

activities.text: If you have time after Nippara, detour to Lake Okutama, a dam surrounded by forest where you can rent a pedal boat or simply stroll on the floating bridge. The Okutama region is also known for its outdoor onsen—perfect for relaxing after a day of walking.

visits.text: Lake Tazawa is Japan's deepest lake, with 423.4 meters below the surface - ranking it 17th globally. Its extreme depth prevents it from freezing in winter, giving it a unique thermal advantage in this snowy region. Its waters vary from lapis lazuli blue to jade green depending on sunlight, and their transparency is striking. The lake is listed among Japan's 100 most beautiful landscapes and is part of Towada-Hachimantai National Park. A drive around the lake takes half an hour without stopping; by bicycle, count on 2 to 3 hours.

The golden statue of Tatsuko, on the lake's western shore, is Tazawa's unmissable visual symbol. Legend tells that Tatsuko was a girl of exceptional beauty who prayed for 100 consecutive nights to preserve her beauty eternally. A deity directed her to a sacred spring in the mountains. She drank so much from it that she was transformed into a dragon and sank to the bottom of the lake to become its guardian. Her lover Hachiro, also transformed into a dragon, comes to visit her every autumn from Lake Hachiro-gata - which explains why the latter freezes in winter when its guardian is absent, while Tazawa always remains ice-free.

activities.text: The sightseeing cruise (40 min round trip) between Shirahama pier and Tatsuko's statue is a good way to see the lake from the water, with audio commentary. By kayak or canoe (available April to November), you can approach the wooded shores much more closely.

Gozanoishi Shrine, on the lake's north shore, is marked by a large vermilion torii at the water's edge. This is where feudal lords once came to draw inspiration from the lake's beauty. Near the shrine is the Katagashira spring - the very spot where Tatsuko reportedly drank too much water to preserve her beauty.

Nyuto Onsen, a hot spring village hidden in forests 20 minutes by bus from the lake, groups seven rustic ryokan around seven distinct springs. The atmosphere is that of a wild mountain bath - some establishments offer day-use access to their open-air baths (rotenburo) directly on the river.

days_plan.itin_title: Tohoku Lakes & Volcanoes Circuit

visits.text: Lake Mashu is a volcanic caldera formed over 7,000 years ago. At 211 meters depth and visibility extending to 20-30 meters in clear weather, it ranks among the world's most transparent lakes - only Lake Baikal can rival it. Its water, fed solely by rain and groundwater (no rivers enter or exit), maintains a remarkably stable level. The Ainu call it "Kamuito" - the lake of the gods. Its deep blue color, called "Mashu blue," varies with light and sun angle. Mist envelops it more than 100 days a year, adding mystical atmosphere. Access only from observatories - the lakeshore is in fully closed special protected zone.

Mount Io (Atosanupuri), a few kilometers away, is an active volcano whose Ainu name means "naked mountain" - and it's the right word. Thousands of fumaroles spew sulfurous steam from the bare flanks, creating a yellow and gray lunar landscape. It's a former sulfur extraction site, abandoned. You can approach on foot from the parking lot.

Lake Kussharo, about fifteen kilometers away, is Japan's largest crater lake. In winter, whooper swans migrate from Siberia and stay in non-frozen zones warmed by geothermal springs. A hot sand bath (Sunayu) is accessible on the south shore - dig the beach sand and a natural onsen gushes.

activities.text: Visit Lake Mashu in clear weather - check local forecast before departure. Observatory No. 1 offers the most direct view of the lake and the volcanic islet Kamuishu at its center. Observatory No. 3, higher up, gives a panoramic view of the entire caldera and Mount Mashu. Observatory No. 1 parking is paid (410 yen). If fog covers the lake, Ainu legend says the show is still worth the trip.

Bathe at Kawayu Onsen, one of Japan's most acidic hot springs (pH between 1.6 and 1.9). This sulfurous water, originating from Mount Io's activity, is renowned for relieving skin problems. Free foot baths (ashiyu) line the town streets - the contrast between boiling water and Hokkaido's fresh air is invigorating.

days_plan.itin_title: Extreme Hokkaido Road Trip: Arctic Nature and Volcanoes

visits.text: Experience the legendary Shibuya Crossing, one of the world's busiest pedestrian intersections where thousands cross simultaneously in a mesmerizing urban ballet. This iconic Tokyo landmark epitomizes the city's electric energy and has become a symbol of modern Japan. Watch the spectacle from above at the nearby Starbucks or dive right into the organized chaos.

Stroll down the elegant Omotesando avenue, Tokyo's answer to the Champs-Élysées, lined with architectural masterpieces housing luxury boutiques and trendy cafés. This tree-lined boulevard showcases cutting-edge Japanese design with buildings by world-renowned architects like Tadao Ando and Kengo Kuma. The sophisticated atmosphere makes it perfect for window shopping and people-watching.

Immerse yourself in the vibrant chaos of Takeshita Street in Harajuku, the epicenter of Japanese youth culture and fashion. This narrow pedestrian street bursts with colorful boutiques, quirky souvenir shops, and unique snack vendors serving rainbow cotton candy and crepes. Witness Tokyo's creative spirit through the eclectic street fashion on display.

Marvel at the magnificent Senso-ji Temple in Asakusa, Tokyo's oldest and most celebrated Buddhist temple dating back to 628 AD. Enter through the iconic Kaminarimon (Thunder Gate) with its massive red lantern, then walk through the bustling Nakamise-dori shopping street leading to the main hall. The temple complex offers a glimpse into Edo-period Tokyo.

Wander through Ueno Park, a cultural haven housing some of Japan's finest museums, a zoo, and beautiful gardens. This expansive park was Japan's first public park and features stunning cherry blossoms in spring and lotus flowers in summer. Visit the Tokyo National Museum to explore the world's largest collection of Japanese art and antiquities.

Discover Kappabashi-dori, Tokyo's famous 'Kitchen Town' stretching nearly a kilometer with shops selling professional cookware, plastic food samples, and restaurant supplies. This unique shopping street offers an authentic glimpse into Japan's culinary culture and is the perfect place to find unique souvenirs like handcrafted knives and realistic food replicas.

Lose yourself in the immersive digital wonderland of teamLab Borderless, a groundbreaking museum where art installations respond to your presence and blend seamlessly into one another. This revolutionary space features rooms of cascading digital waterfalls, infinite crystal universes, and interactive flower gardens that create unforgettable sensory experiences. Allow at least two hours to fully explore this boundary-pushing exhibition.

Ascend Tokyo Tower, the iconic 333-meter communications tower inspired by the Eiffel Tower and painted in distinctive red and white. Built in 1958, this beloved landmark offers panoramic observation decks with sweeping views across the Tokyo skyline to Mount Fuji on clear days. The tower remains a nostalgic symbol of Japan's post-war modernization.

Explore the peaceful grounds of Zojo-ji Temple, one of Tokyo's most significant Buddhist temples and the family temple of the Tokugawa shoguns. Located at the foot of Tokyo Tower, this impressive temple complex features the magnificent Sangedatsumon gate, one of the oldest wooden structures in Tokyo. The juxtaposition of ancient temple and modern tower creates iconic photo opportunities.

Step back in time in Kawagoe, the 'Little Edo' town preserving the atmosphere of old Tokyo with its stunning Kurazukuri warehouses along the historic street. These clay-walled merchant buildings date back to the Edo period and now house traditional shops, sweet stores, and craft boutiques. The iconic Bell Tower (Toki no Kane) has been chiming the hours for over 400 years.

Visit the historic Kitain Temple in Kawagoe, a significant Buddhist temple housing the only surviving structures from Edo Castle, including chambers once used by the third Tokugawa shogun. The temple grounds feature 500 unique Rakan statues, each depicting a disciple of Buddha with distinct expressions. The beautiful gardens are particularly stunning during autumn foliage season.

Explore the Kawagoe Hikawa Shrine, a 1,500-year-old Shinto sanctuary famous for its rituals dedicated to love and relationships. The shrine features a charming tunnel of wind chimes in summer and offers unique sea bream-shaped fortune slips. Couples come from across Japan to pray for romantic success and marital happiness.

Stand in awe before the Great Buddha of Kamakura (Daibutsu), a magnificent 13-meter bronze statue that has sat in peaceful meditation since 1252. This iconic outdoor Buddha at Kotoku-in Temple survived tsunamis, earthquakes, and typhoons that destroyed its original wooden hall. Visitors can even enter the hollow statue to appreciate its remarkable construction.

Explore the beautiful Hase-dera Temple, perched on a hillside offering stunning views of Sagami Bay and housing a magnificent 9-meter gilded statue of Kannon, goddess of mercy. The temple grounds feature peaceful gardens, thousands of small Jizo statues, and cave tunnels carved with Buddhist images. The observation platform provides one of Kamakura's most beautiful panoramas.

Hike through the atmospheric trails connecting Kamakura's many temples, including the mystical Zeniarai Benten shrine hidden in a cave where visitors wash money to multiply their fortune. The forested paths lead past mossy stone statues, bamboo groves, and intimate shrines rarely visited by tourists. The spiritual atmosphere of ancient Kamakura rivals that of Kyoto.

activities.text: Find serenity at the magnificent Meiji Shrine, a Shinto sanctuary dedicated to Emperor Meiji and Empress Shoken, nestled within a lush 170-acre forest in the heart of Tokyo. Walk through the towering torii gates and along peaceful gravel paths that transport you from urban chaos to spiritual tranquility. The shrine offers authentic Shinto rituals and traditional wedding ceremonies.

Relax in Yoyogi Park, one of Tokyo's largest green spaces offering a peaceful retreat from the bustling city. This beloved urban oasis attracts joggers, picnickers, and street performers, especially on weekends when you might encounter rockabilly dancers and musicians. The park connects Harajuku to Shibuya, making it perfect for a leisurely afternoon stroll.

Explore the atmospheric alleys of Golden Gai in Shinjuku, a labyrinth of over 200 tiny bars crammed into six narrow lanes. This nostalgic entertainment district has survived since the post-war era, preserving old Tokyo's intimate drinking culture. Each bar seats only a handful of patrons, creating unique and personal experiences.

Dive into the electric world of Akihabara, Tokyo's legendary electronics and anime district where neon signs illuminate multi-story gaming arcades and manga shops. This otaku paradise offers everything from vintage video games to cutting-edge technology, plus unique experiences like maid cafés and the massive Gachapon Hall filled with capsule toy machines.

Browse the vibrant Ameya-Yokocho market stretching under the JR railway tracks between Ueno and Okachimachi stations. This bustling open-air market offers incredible deals on everything from fresh seafood and produce to clothing and cosmetics. The energetic atmosphere and vendors calling out their prices create an authentic Japanese market experience.

Visit the historic Kanda Myojin Shrine, a serene Shinto sanctuary just minutes from Akihabara's chaos, famous for blessing electronics and ensuring business success. This 1,300-year-old shrine attracts both traditional worshippers and anime fans, with unique ema (votive tablets) often decorated with manga-style artwork. The contrast with nearby Akihabara is striking.

Discover Roppongi Hills, a massive urban development featuring world-class art at the Mori Art Museum and breathtaking views from the Tokyo City View observation deck on the 52nd floor. This complex represents modern Tokyo at its finest, with cutting-edge architecture, upscale shopping, and sophisticated dining options. The outdoor Sky Deck offers unobstructed 360-degree views.

Stroll through the trendy neighborhood of Daikanyama, Tokyo's answer to Brooklyn, known for its independent boutiques, design shops, and the stunning Daikanyama T-Site bookstore complex. This sophisticated area attracts creative professionals and offers a more relaxed pace than central Tokyo. The architectural beauty and curated shops make it a haven for design enthusiasts.

Walk along the picturesque Meguro River in Nakameguro, one of Tokyo's most charming neighborhoods famous for its cherry blossom-lined banks and hip café culture. The river is flanked by trendy boutiques, vintage shops, and cozy restaurants that spill onto the waterfront. During hanami season, the tunnel of pink blossoms creates an unforgettable spectacle.

Wander down Taisho-roman Street in Kawagoe, where early 20th-century Western-influenced architecture creates a nostalgic atmosphere reminiscent of the Taisho era. The street features charming retro cafés, antique shops, and buildings that blend Japanese and European design elements. This photogenic area offers a unique contrast to the Edo-period warehouse district.

Experience the urban excitement of Ikebukuro's Sunshine City, a massive entertainment complex featuring an aquarium, planetarium, observation deck, and extensive shopping. The Sunshine 60 building was once Japan's tallest skyscraper and still offers impressive views from its observation floor. The complex caters to anime fans with numerous themed stores and attractions.

Ascend to the Sunshine 60 Observatory for panoramic views across Tokyo's endless urban landscape from the 60th floor. On clear days, the vista extends to Mount Fuji and the surrounding mountains. The observation deck features interactive exhibits and romantic sunset viewing experiences.

Discover Yokohama's futuristic Minato Mirai 21 district, a stunning waterfront development featuring the landmark Yokohama Landmark Tower and the historic red-brick warehouses converted into trendy shops. The area offers spectacular bay views, world-class museums, and the Cosmo Clock 21, one of the world's largest Ferris wheels. The blend of modern architecture and maritime heritage is uniquely Yokohama.

Stroll through Yamashita Park, Yokohama's beloved seaside promenade stretching along the harbor with views of the iconic Bay Bridge and historic ships. This beautifully landscaped waterfront park features rose gardens, public art, and the retired passenger liner Hikawa Maru. The park connects the city's major attractions and offers a peaceful escape from urban bustle.

Immerse yourself in Yokohama's Chinatown, the largest in Japan and one of the largest in the world, with over 500 shops and restaurants packed into colorful streets. The ornate gates and Chinese architecture create a vibrant atmosphere filled with the aromas of steaming dim sum and sizzling street food. Don't miss the Kanteibyo Temple, a stunning shrine dedicated to the Chinese god of prosperity.

visits.text: The Unkai Terrace at Hoshino Resorts Tomamu, opened in 2006, perches at 1,088 meters altitude. "Unkai" means "sea of clouds" in Japanese - the phenomenon occurs when precise conditions converge: clear night, rapid nocturnal cooling in the valley, humid air condensing at ground level while heights remain clear. The probability of observing the sea of clouds is about 30 to 40% during the opening period (May to mid-October). The terrace offers six observation points including the Cloud Walk (cloud-shaped walkway jutting into the void), a Cloud Pool, and a Cloud Bar.

activities.text: Wake before dawn - the gondola begins rotations around 5am in high season. Sea of clouds probability is highest on mornings following a warm sunny day preceded by a cool night, typically July to September. The site publishes a next-morning forecast each noon on its official website. Bring extra layers: it's noticeably colder at 1,088 m than on the plain, even in midsummer.

On the Unkai Terrace, three types of sea of clouds are possible depending on conditions: the "Pacific sea of clouds" carried by marine flow, the "Tomamu sea of clouds" by radiative cooling, and the "bad weather sea of clouds" during disturbances. A cloud guide (Kumokai Guide) explains in real time the observable phenomenon that morning.

If the sea of clouds is absent that morning, the view of the Hidaka and Tokachi mountains from 1,088 meters remains beautiful. Descending, explore the Hoshino Resorts domain which offers outdoor summer activities, horseback riding, and hot air ballooning above the meadows.

places.name: Towada

visits.text: The Oirase Gorge is the only watercourse that escapes from Lake Towada, running 14 km from Nenokuchi to Yakeyama. Classified as a Special Scenic Site and National Natural Monument, the gorge is nicknamed 'the road of waterfalls': a dozen falls pour directly into the stream from the gorge walls, some from dizzying heights. Choshi Otaki waterfall - 7 meters high and 20 meters wide - is the most emblematic: fish cannot swim beyond it, which means Lake Towada has never had perennial bottom-dwelling fish. The marked trail that follows the water on foot is one of Japan's most pleasant.

Lake Towada is one of Japan's largest caldera lakes, formed by volcanic activity that began 200,000 years ago. Its deep blue waters are surrounded by wooded cliffs that plunge directly into the lake. Towada Shrine, founded according to tradition in 807 by Sakagami Tamuramaro, is built on the peninsula that juts into the lake - one of Tohoku's most mystical places.

activities.text: The best strategy for the Oirase Gorge is to walk from Ishigedo (mid-route) toward Nenokuchi, then take the bus back - or vice versa. You can also rent a bike at the gorge entrance. The fresh greenery from mid-May to early June is the most beautiful season, but the autumn colors (mid-October to early November) rival in intensity. In winter, the waterfalls freeze into spectacular blue ice columns (guided tours required).

The Lake Towada cruise (50 min between Yasumiya and Nenokuchi) is an excellent complement to the gorge hike. From the boat, you see the beech and birch forests descending to the water. In autumn, the reflection of maples in the lake creates a tableau that alone justifies the detour. The statue of two women facing each other (work by Kotaro Takamura, 1953) stands at the lake edge at Yasumiya.

visits.text: Tsutanuma Pond is one of seven marshes of Tsuta Nananuma, nestled in a virgin forest of Japanese beech at the foot of the Hakkoda Mountains. The Tsuta Onsen, a ryokan adjoining the marshes, has been documented since the year 1147 (Heian period) - over 850 years of hot spring history. The spring surges directly beneath the baths, never touching air between the source and the tub - an absolute rarity in Japan. The Numa Meguri no Komichi trail (2.8 km) links six of the seven marshes in two hours of leisurely walking under the beeches.

Tsutanuma is nationally famous for its sunrise spectacle in autumn (typically mid to late October): the morning's slanting light illuminates the beeches and red maples on the opposite shore, which reflect in the perfectly still water of the marsh. This spectacle exists only in windless weather, making it unpredictable and all the more precious. Reservations are now required to access the site during peak season (tickets in advance).

activities.text: Arrive at Tsutanuma marsh at dawn if you're in full autumn season - the observation platform on the shore gets crowded after 7am. The scene lasts only a few minutes after sunrise, until the light rises and the wind picks up. Outside autumn, the marsh is calm and beautiful in all seasons, with birds nesting in June.

The Hakkoda Mountains, 20 minutes away, are a volcanic massif of sixteen peaks (highest point Odake at 1,585 m) with alpine wetlands and trails for all levels. Sukayu Onsen, founded in 1684, is one of Tohoku's most renowned onsen with its large mixed-gender cypress wood bath for 1,000 people (senninburo). It's also one of Japan's snowiest regions in winter.

The Hakkoda-Towada Gold Line road, which connects Sukayu Onsen to Lake Towada, is one of northern Japan's most beautiful roads in autumn. Tunnels of yellow and red trees overhang the road for kilometers.

days_plan.itin_title: Tohoku Lakes & Volcanoes Circuit

visits.text: The Goshikinuma ponds - 'five colored ponds' - are the inadvertent gift of a volcanic catastrophe. In July 1888, Mount Bandai (1,816 m) exploded in a devastating eruption, burying several villages and killing hundreds. The debris dammed the rivers and created more than 300 lakes and ponds on the highlands behind the mountain. These lakes then absorbed volcanic minerals which, depending on each pond's composition, refract light differently and produce colors ranging from emerald green to turquoise to cobalt blue. A marked 4 km trail links the main ponds - count on an hour on foot, more if you stop to photograph.

Bishamonnuma is the largest pond (4 km perimeter) and the only one where you can rent a rowboat to paddle across emerald-colored water with Mount Bandai as backdrop. From the observation bridge built 5 minutes' walk from the visitor center, the view of the pond and mountain is the most photographed in the entire region. The Akanuma, Rurinuma, and Aonuma ponds offer different hues depending on the hour and light - the best light comes in the morning before the tour buses arrive.

activities.text: The Mount Bandai Eruption Memorial Museum, located in Urabandai itself, offers a 'sonic body' simulation that recreates the 1888 eruption - an astonishing experience for understanding how this improbable landscape was born in a single day. An adjacent 3D theater presents a film about the mountain.

The hike from the Goshikinuma trail to the Bandai summit takes 3 to 4 hours one way - the trailhead starts from the western end of the Goshikinuma path. You climb through a lunar landscape of volcanic rocks, with the colored ponds below and the Tohoku Alps on the horizon. Bring good shoes and a waterproof layer.

Urabandai's colors are as spectacular in autumn as in spring: maples and beeches cover the shores in a gradient of red, orange, and gold from mid-October to early November. The region is also popular for skiing in winter and fishing in the lakes in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Tohoku Lakes & Volcanoes Circuit

visits.text: The ruins of Katsuren Castle, a UNESCO site, crown the Katsuren Peninsula from a hill 100 meters high. In the 15th century, Lord Amawari made it a commercial center rivaling Shuri through maritime trade with China and Southeast Asia. Too ambitious, he attempted to overthrow King Shō Taikyu and was defeated in 1458. Roman coins were found here—a first in Japan. From the summit, the 360-degree view across the Pacific and East China Sea is among Okinawa's finest.

The Kaichu Road (Kaichu-doro) is a scenic 5-km road built over the sea, connecting the Katsuren Peninsula to the islands of Henza, Miyagi, Hamahiga, and Ikei. Free to cross, it offers the exhilarating sensation of driving over the emerald ocean. Midway, the Ayahashi Station (shaped like a ship) has local products and a small maritime museum.

The ruins of Nakagusuku Castle, also a UNESCO site, possess the best-preserved stone walls of all Okinawan gusuku. Built with perfectly fitted masonry technique, the walls follow the natural terrain with elegant curves. The view embraces both the Pacific and East China Sea.
Bios no Oka is a 75,000-square-meter subtropical park where you can take a 25-minute boat cruise on Lake Nuchi-unai through a forest of tree ferns and vines. Kids love the water buffalo and canoes.

activities.text: Drive across the islands via Kaichu Road: Henza for its small fishing port, Hamahiga for the sacred Shirumichu shrine linked to the goddess Amamikiyo, and Ikei for its deserted beaches. Allow half a day for all four islands.
Visit the Amawari Park Museum at the base of Katsuren Castle—opened in 2021, it displays excavated objects, including the famous Roman coins, and animations about Lord Amawari's history.

days_plan.itin_title: Okinawa and Ryukyu Islands Journey: Beaches and Culture

visits.text: The Soya Hills, behind Cape Soya, are a periglacial undulating landscape formed 20,000 years ago during the last ice age by repeated cycles of ground freezing and thawing. Their rolling form, devoid of trees since fires in the mid-Meiji era, has been classified Hokkaido heritage since 2004. The 57 wind turbines turning there have powered Wakkanai city with electricity for several years. The White Road (Shiro no Michi), opened in 2011, winds for 3 kilometers through these hills - paved with crushed scallop shells, a local specialty, giving it a dazzling white hue contrasting with the grass green.

Cape Soya is Japan's northernmost accessible point - latitude 45.31°N. 43 km north, in clear weather, you can make out the Russian island of Sakhalin. A triangular monument points north, and a statue honors Mamiya Rinzo, the Japanese explorer who mapped Sakhalin in the 19th century. Cape Soya is also linked to France: Comte de La Pérouse was the first European to navigate between Sakhalin and Hokkaido during his worldwide expedition in the 1780s.

Wakkanai's Breakwater Dome, built in 1936, is a 427-meter-long structure supported by 70 Greek-style columns 13.6 meters high - a baroque architectural feat in this far-north context. It protects the port against waves from Siberia and serves as a panoramic promenade facing the sea and the islands of Rishiri and Rebun.

Return ferry day from Rebun (or Rishiri) to Wakkanai. The crossing from Rebun takes about 2 hours; from Rishiri, 1h40. Wakkanai has an airport with flights to Tokyo (2h) and Sapporo (1h), as well as a JR station with trains to Sapporo (5h30). For travelers ending their road trip here, it's the car return point - or the opportunity to take the scenic train southward.

activities.text: Walk the White Road through the Soya Hills. The trail exists in 3 km version (about 1h) or 5 km (about 2h). Choose a clear day to enjoy the panorama of the Sea of Japan on one side and the Sea of Okhotsk on the other. Wind is constant and sometimes violent - bring a windbreaker. Soya mountain cattle graze peacefully among the wind turbines.

From Cape Noshappu, 10 minutes by bus from Wakkanai, watch the sunset over the Sea of Japan with the silhouettes of Rishiri and Rebun islands in the background - Rishiri Island with its conical volcano resembles a northern Fuji. It's one of northern Hokkaido's most appreciated twilight spectacles.

Wakkanai is at Russia's edge - Cyrillic signs intersperse downtown, recalling Russian territorial claims on the Kuril Islands. Taste specialties from Fukuko covered market: crab, grilled scallops, and hotate ramen (scallop ramen), local specialty.

If you have time before departure, head down to Fukuko Ichiba covered market for a final far-north seafood meal: crabs, sea urchins, and fresh Sea of Okhotsk scallops. Hotate ramen (scallop ramen) is the local specialty to order.

For the most adventurous travelers: Teuri Island, 195 km south (Haboro), is accessible by ferry from Haboro port (1h30 crossing). It shelters the world's largest rhinoceros auklet colony - 400,000 breeding pairs. Nocturnal June visits allow observing birds returning to their burrows at sunset. Plan a night in Haboro or on the island. This detour is independent of the rest of the itinerary.

visits.text: Yokohama's Red Brick Warehouses (Akarenga Sōko), built in 1911 and 1913, are among the last vestiges of the port opened to international trade in 1859. Today converted into galleries, shops, and restaurants, they form a very pleasant waterfront for strolling. In the evening, lighting highlights the brick facades with the illuminated Bay Bridge as backdrop. In winter, a European-inspired Christmas market sets up here.

Take the ferry from Mikasa Park (Yokosuka, 30 minutes south of Yokohama by train) to Sarushima Island, the only uninhabited natural island in Tokyo Bay. The crossing takes 10 minutes. On the island, moss-covered brick tunnels and abandoned bunkers dating from the Meiji era give a post-apocalyptic atmosphere. From late September to early November, golden pampas grass (susuki) covers the island's heights—a startling spectacle just offshore from Tokyo.

activities.text: Returning to Yokohama, tour Chinatown (Chūkagai)—Japan's largest Chinese quarter, with over 500 restaurants and shops spread across 10 streets. Nikuman (steamed meat buns) are sold on every corner. If you have time, ascend Yokohama Landmark Tower (296m), whose observatory offers views of the bay and, on clear days, Mount Fuji.

days_plan.itin_title: Kanto Flower & Landscape Journey

places.name: Yokote

visits.text: The Yokote Kamakura Festival is one of Japan's most touching winter celebrations, with nearly 450 years of history. Every February 15th and 16th, more than 80 igloos (kamakura) 2 meters high and a hundred mini-kamakura illuminated by candles fill the city streets. The tradition originally arose from a religious ceremony: niches carved in the snow around neighborhood wells to honor the water deity and pray for abundant harvests and family safety.

The igloos' interiors are furnished with bamboo mats and an altar to the water deity, accompanied by offerings of sake and mochi. Neighborhood children invite visitors to enter, offering them warm amazake (sweet non-alcoholic sake) and grilled rice cakes. At 6pm, thousands of mini-kamakura light up along the banks of the Yokote River and at Minami School Park - a display of soft, intimate light that lasts until 9pm.

activities.text: Building a mini-kamakura is an activity offered at certain locations during the festival - you learn the techniques of packing and carving snow under locals' supervision. The grounds of Yokote Castle (rebuilt in concrete in 1965) stay open specially during the festival from 10am to 9pm, offering a rare nighttime view of the city from the tower.

Outside festival season (the rest of the year), you can still see kamakura preserved in artificial cold at Yokote's Kamakurakan Hall - two permanent igloos in a cold room, open year-round. Yokote is also known for Yokote yakisoba (stir-fried noodles topped with Worcestershire sauce and crowned with a fried egg), one of Tohoku's most popular B-class gourmet specialties.

visits.text: Lake Okama is one of Tohoku's most spectacular natural sites: a volcanic crater lake perched at 1,672 meters altitude, in the heart of the Mount Zao massif (highest peak at 1,841 m, on the Yamagata-Miyagi border). Its name means 'the cauldron' in Japanese, referring to the round, deep shape of the crater. The lake measures 325 meters in diameter and 27.6 meters deep - its water is so acidic that neither fish nor plants can survive in it, which partly explains its surreal colors. The Japanese also call it Goshiki-numa (five colors) because its waters shift from emerald green to turquoise depending on light, time, and weather. Arrive in clear weather to see the true colors: in bad weather or fog, the effect is greatly diminished.

To reach Okama, take the Zao Echo Line road to the Kattadake parking area (1,758 m) - 5 minutes' walk from the crater rim. The Zao Highline, the final toll section (~600 yen), is open from late April to early November. At the Katta summit, don't miss the small Shinto shrine dedicated to Zao Gongen: mountaineers make a brief stop here before walking along the crater rim. The lake closes in winter - if you're here off-season, Zao Onsen and its juhyo (Day 4) are the main attraction.

activities.text: The Umanose trail ('horse's back,' 1h round trip from the main viewpoint) runs along the crater rim to Kumanodake summit. It's the only way to see the lake from multiple angles and observe how the water's colors change with the light angle. Caution: the trail crosses unstable volcanic terrain with steep elevation changes - wear closed shoes.

Late in the day, descend toward Zao Onsen (10 km) for your overnight stay. The Dai-Rotenburo, a large public open-air bath with milky sulfurous waters, stays open until evening - an invigorating onsen after a day at altitude, with views of the wooded ridges of the massif.

places.name: Zao Onsen

visits.text: The juhyo of Zao - literally 'ice trees' - have become one of winter's most iconic images in Japan. These are Aomori fir trees (Abies mariesii) covered with a layer of ice blown by Siberian winds, then buried under snow, which end up resembling strange white creatures emerging from the mist. They appear from January to mid-March, with a peak typically around mid-February. The ropeway climbs to 1,660 meters from Zao Onsen up to Jizô Sancho Station - from there, the ice monsters extend for several kilometers on foot.

The Zao massif is also one of Tohoku's best ski resorts, with 28 runs and several lifts. Outside ski season (December-March), the mountain offers pleasant hiking with alpine lakes and wetland landscapes. At Zao Onsen itself, the Dai-Rotenburo public bath is a large open-air bath where the steaming sulfurous waters have a distinctive milky tint - one of the highest sulfuric acid concentrations of any onsen in Japan.

activities.text: The nighttime illumination of the juhyo runs every evening in winter from the ropeway station (check seasonal schedules). Colored spotlights transform the ice monsters into a fairy-tale setting - one of Japan's most surprising winter spectacles. Dress warmly: temperatures at the summit regularly drop below -15°C.

Outside winter season, Zao Onsen offers hikes to ridges and alpine wetlands. The Dai-Rotenburo - large open-air bath with white sulfurous waters - is one of Tohoku's most spectacular onsen. Several inns offer day-use access. Plan to linger in the evening: the steam in the cold mountain air creates a particular atmosphere.
